decision.title: docs/ is GSD upstream â€” archive it, don't rewrite it
decision.summary: User review 2026-04-10: "these docs do not describe gad, they describe GSD, which GAD is based off of, this is a fork of GSD." The docs/ directory (62 files referencing GSD across ARCHITECTURE.md, AGENTS.md, COMMANDS.md, CLI-TOOLS.md, CONFIGURATION.md, USER-GUIDE.md, FEATURES.md, context-budget.md, context-monitor.md, workflow-discuss-mode.md, superpowers/, and 4 language translations) is GSD upstream documentation that shipped with the fork. NONE of it describes GAD. Policy: (1) Move docs/ to docs-gsd-upstream/ as an explicit archive with a README noting it's prior art from the upstream fork, not GAD documentation. (2) GAD's documentation is the SITE (get-anything-done.vercel.app) â€” /methodology, /standards, /decisions, /glossary, /contribute, /skeptic, etc. We do NOT maintain a parallel docs/ folder. (3) The GSD docs are valuable as reference for what the upstream was doing (cited on /lineage), and some content may inspire future GAD features â€” but we evaluate those features through our eval framework rather than inherit GSD's claims. (4) The GSD agent patterns (18 specialized gsd-* agents described in docs/AGENTS.md) are useful prior art for the agent-parallelization hypothesis (gad-93).
decision.impact: (1) Quick rename: git mv docs/ docs-gsd-upstream/. Add a README.md at the top explaining the provenance. (2) Any site page currently linking to docs/ files updates to docs-gsd-upstream/. (3) The prebuild does NOT scan docs/ for anything â€” confirmed by grep. (4) No site content changes needed â€” the site is self-contained.

decision.title: Agent-parallelization hypothesis â€” does multi-agent work improve outcomes or just burn tokens?
decision.summary: User question 2026-04-10: "we would want to test if putting [agents] in and parallelizing the work specifically speed up development at the least, use more tokens vs less tokens if we didn't use multiple agents." New research hypothesis: the GSD-inherited multi-agent model (18 specialized agents with distinct roles â€” researcher, planner, executor, verifier, debugger, etc.) may improve development speed or quality by parallelizing work. BUT it may also increase token consumption without proportional quality improvement. The GAD framework already ships 21 gad-* agents in the agents/ directory â€” they're available but their impact on eval outcomes has never been measured. We need a test condition where agents are explicitly spawned as subagents (via Claude Code's Agent tool or Codex's parallel-task mechanism) vs a control where the same work is done serially by a single agent. Metrics to capture: total_tokens, duration_ms, tool_use_count, subagent_spawn_count (already in trace v4 schema via gad-50), and human_review quality score. The hypothesis is falsified if multi-agent runs use significantly more tokens for the same or lower quality. It is confirmed if multi-agent runs are faster AND at least equal quality. This is a new line on the hypothesis tracks chart.
decision.impact: (1) New eval condition queued for future rounds: "escape-the-dungeon-multi-agent" â€” same requirements, same workflow (GAD), but the AGENTS.md explicitly instructs the agent to spawn subagents for parallelizable tasks (e.g. planning and implementation phases run as separate subagents). (2) Metrics already capturable: subagent_spawn_count in TRACE.json from trace v4 hooks. token comparison is the key signal. (3) This ties to the broader question of whether the GSD upstream's 18-agent model (gsd-project-researcher, gsd-planner, gsd-executor, etc.) was beneficial or overhead. GAD inherited the agents but hasn't tested whether they help. (4) The hypothesis tracks chart gains a new dashed line (multi-agent, planned). (5) The eval template matrix gains a new column for the multi-agent condition: same as GAD but with subagent-spawn instructions in AGENTS.md.

decision.title: .agents/ convention extended â€” agents/ and commands/ should follow the same cross-client pattern as skills/
decision.summary: User observation 2026-04-10: "if we are canonically putting skills in our .agents folder and checking in that repo, then should we not do the same for agents and commands?" Currently: skills live at .agents/skills/ (agentskills.io convention per gad-80), agents live at agents/ (repo root), commands live at commands/gad/ (repo root). The inconsistency means install.js has different copy paths for each artifact type. Policy: (1) Long-term, agents and commands should also live under .agents/ for consistency â€” .agents/agents/ and .agents/commands/. (2) Short-term, this is NOT a blocking migration â€” the agentskills.io standard only covers skills, not agents or commands. Agents have a different loading mechanism (they're not progressive-disclosure catalog items, they're spawn targets). Commands are Claude-Code-specific slash commands. (3) When we migrate: git mv agents/ .agents/agents/, git mv commands/gad/ .agents/commands/, update install.js and prebuild scanners. Queued as a follow-up â€” not done this turn. (4) The user's broader point is about consistency and discoverability. A new user opening the repo should find everything agent-related under one tree (.agents/) rather than scattered across root directories.
decision.impact: (1) Queued migration: agents/ â†’ .agents/agents/, commands/gad/ â†’ .agents/commands/. Not blocking. (2) install.js already handles both separately â€” the migration is a path change, not a logic change. (3) The prebuild scans agents/ and commands/gad/ with their own loops â€” update the constants when migrating. (4) This does NOT affect how Claude Code or other runtimes discover agents â€” they load from .claude/agents/ or similar runtime-specific paths. The .agents/ convention is the SOURCE location in the repo, not the runtime location.

decision.title: CLI-as-a-variable â€” GAD skills that use the CLI need the CLI installed in the eval template
decision.summary: User observation 2026-04-10: "are we including the CLI in these templates. if the gad skills are using the gad cli, then that must also be used to fully implement GAD. just using GAD artifacts is one thing also, adding the CLI and snapshot is something I want to test as well." This is correct: several GAD skills (execute-phase, plan-phase, verify-phase, task-checkpoint, snapshot-optimize) invoke `gad` CLI commands like `gad snapshot`, `gad state`, `gad tasks`. If those skills are in the template but the CLI is not installed, the skills fail silently. The CLI is CURRENTLY available in eval worktrees (because the worktree is a git checkout of the same repo that contains `bin/gad.cjs`), but it's not DOCUMENTED as a deliberate inclusion. Policy: (1) For the GAD and GAD+Emergent conditions, the CLI is explicitly allowed and skills may invoke it. (2) For the Planning-only condition, the CLI is NOT included â€” the agent has the XML artifacts but not the tooling. This tests whether the artifacts alone help. (3) For Bare and Emergent conditions, the CLI is NOT available. (4) A future 6th condition â€” "CLI-only" (CLI + snapshot + no skills, no planning artifacts) â€” could test whether just the CLI loop (`gad snapshot` â†’ `gad state` â†’ work â†’ commit) helps without any of the planning XML or skill methodology. Queued as a possible round-6 addition.
decision.impact: (1) GAD template's AGENTS.md should explicitly tell the agent to use `gad snapshot` and `gad state` commands. (2) Planning-only template's AGENTS.md should NOT mention the CLI. (3) The template matrix table on /methodology gains a new row: "GAD CLI available". (4) Future 6th condition (CLI-only) deferred to planning â€” not scaffolded yet. (5) The existing eval worktrees already have the CLI available via `bin/gad.cjs` in the git checkout, so no file-copy change is needed â€” just documentation clarity about what's allowed per condition.

decision.title: Greenfield â†’ brownfield lineage model â€” branching visualization + same 5 hypotheses retested
decision.summary: User directive 2026-04-10: "we want to show how we take the source from a greenfield and then run some brownfield evaluations on it. when we do that we want to represent that as branching like git branching or mermaid graphs." Formalizes the greenfield-to-brownfield relationship: (1) Every brownfield eval project references a specific greenfield run as its baseline via `gad.json baseline: { project, version, source }`. The source path points at the preserved run's code directory. (2) The site's hypothesis-tracks chart must distinguish greenfield from brownfield â€” either as separate charts, a toggle, or a layered view. Greenfield rounds are the trunk. Brownfield rounds branch off from a specific greenfield run's output. (3) The same 5-condition matrix (bare / planning-only / GAD / emergent / GAD+emergent) applies to brownfield work â€” testing the same hypotheses but for CODE EXTENSION instead of greenfield creation. This is the generalization axis the freedom hypothesis needs: maybe bare wins greenfield but GAD wins brownfield (where planning helps navigate an existing codebase). (4) Brownfield eval runs are associated with their greenfield predecessor per domain. The branching visualization shows this lineage: "Bare v3 (greenfield, round 3, 0.70) â†’ branches into Brownfield-Bare v1, Brownfield-Emergent v1, Brownfield-GAD v1." (5) Any greenfield run can become a brownfield baseline â€” not just the best-scoring one. When the user selects a round on the hypothesis chart, the brownfield branches from that round should be visible.
decision.impact: (1) The existing brownfield eval projects (etd-brownfield-bare, etd-brownfield-emergent, etd-brownfield-gad) already reference bare v3 as baseline â€” the data model works. (2) New site component: EvalLineageGraph rendering a directed graph (greenfield runs as nodes â†’ brownfield runs as branches). Could use mermaid-js, a custom SVG, or recharts tree. (3) The hypothesis-tracks chart needs a greenfield/brownfield mode selector â€” when viewing greenfield, it shows the existing line chart; when viewing brownfield, it shows the branching results. (4) For round 5: after the 5 greenfield conditions run, the best-scoring greenfield run(s) become the baseline for round 5's brownfield conditions. This means brownfield round 5 DEPENDS on greenfield round 5 completing first. (5) The 5-condition brownfield matrix is: brownfield-bare (no skills, extend existing code), brownfield-planning-only (planning XML, extend code), brownfield-GAD (full framework, extend code), brownfield-emergent (inherited skills, extend code), brownfield-GAD-emergent (everything, extend code). Some of these don't exist yet as projects â€” scaffold them when greenfield round 5 results are in. (6) /roadmap page gains a mermaid-style graph showing the lineage across rounds.

decision.title: Adopt npx skills (vercel-labs/skills) as the canonical user-facing skill installer
decision.summary: User directive 2026-04-09: "find skills can just reuse the npx skill.sh and gad can really just wrap around that making the calls underneath just so gad is like a barrel for tools a user would want and we also would need." vercel-labs/skills (`npx skills`) is the CLI for the open agent skills ecosystem â€” one command that supports 40+ coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Cline, OpenCode, Copilot, Continue, and more) and handles add/list/find/remove/update/check. It respects the `.agents/skills/` convention from gad-80 and works with GitHub shorthand (`owner/repo`), full URLs, and local paths. Policy: GAD does NOT reimplement skill installation. Users install GAD's workspace skills via `npx skills add MagicbornStudios/get-anything-done`. GAD's own `find-skills` skill becomes a thin methodology wrapper around `npx skills find`. The workspace-skill copy pass I added to `install.js` in task 22-46 remains in place as a fallback for users who prefer one-command install via `gad install`, but the canonical recommendation on /contribute + /standards is `npx skills`. The effect is that GAD becomes "a barrel for tools a user would want" â€” we aggregate skill research, methodology, hypothesis testing, and eval framework, and delegate skill installation to the open ecosystem tool that already solved the distribution problem.
decision.impact: (1) The `find-skills` skill (new this turn per gad-73 triumvirate) is authored as a methodology wrapper around `npx skills find`. It does NOT implement its own search logic. (2) /contribute updated (queued) to tell users "install GAD skills via `npx skills add MagicbornStudios/get-anything-done`" as the first-class path. (3) /standards updated (queued) to cite vercel-labs/skills alongside the Anthropic PDF and agentskills.io. (4) The install.js workspace-skills copy (22-46) is retained but labelled as fallback â€” the canonical path is npx skills. (5) /security page gains a new mitigation: skills installed via npx skills inherit the ecosystem's trust posture (public repos, git provenance, hash-pinning). (6) Substantial simplification â€” GAD stops competing with the distribution tool and focuses on what it actually does (skill methodology + evaluation framework + hypothesis testing).

decision.title: Skill graduation criterion â€” no evaluation = experimental
decision.summary: User answer 2026-04-09 to discussion Q2: "they become canonical if they become useful, right now skills that dont have evaluations are considered experimental. also emergent skills stay in the gad repo forever." Formalizes the skill lifecycle: (1) A skill with no evals/evals.json + no benchmark run against it is EXPERIMENTAL, regardless of origin. (2) A skill with a passing with_skill-vs-without_skill benchmark (per gad-81 + the agentskills.io methodology) where `delta.pass_rate > 0` is GRADUATED to canonical. (3) Emergent skills (those under `.agents/skills/emergent/` with `origin: emergent` frontmatter, authored by an agent rather than explicit human intent) STAY emergent forever â€” even if they graduate on the effectiveness axis, they keep the emergent label as a provenance signal. Graduation for emergent skills means "the skill is proven useful" not "the skill moves out of emergent." This matches the user's direction and also aligns with the CSH research framing: emergent skills are the compounding test variable and mixing them with human-authored canonical skills would contaminate the measurement. A `status: experimental | canonical` field is added to every SKILL.md frontmatter starting with this decision. Skills without the field default to experimental.
decision.impact: (1) Every SKILL.md frontmatter gains a `status: experimental | canonical` field. All 27 current workspace skills start as experimental because none have evals/evals.json authored yet. (2) Prebuild scanCatalog() extracts the status field and displays it as a badge on the /skills index + detail pages. (3) The /skills index gains a new filter tab: "experimental" vs "canonical." New users looking at the catalog can filter for battle-tested skills. (4) Graduation is a reviewable action â€” once a skill's benchmark shows `delta.pass_rate > 0`, a decision entry documents the graduation and the frontmatter flips. (5) Emergent skills keep `origin: emergent` even after graduation. The two fields are orthogonal: origin tracks authorship lineage, status tracks evaluation evidence. (6) The SKEPTIC cross-cutting critique about "we call things hypotheses without evidence" gets an orthogonal fix for skills specifically: experimental vs canonical is the skill-level version of preliminary observation vs finding.

decision.title: Per-skill eval harness â€” GAD-native, not skill-creator's Python harness
decision.summary: User directive 2026-04-09: "also note on the skill-creator and its eval script with python. it didnt work for gad's workflow methodology. claude gave a 4/8 tasks 2 times and I think it was testing tool calls. anyway we want to avoid that and have actionable testable frameworks." The anthropic skill-creator ships a Python eval harness (`eval-viewer/generate_review.py` + `scripts/aggregate_benchmark`) that spawns parallel subagent runs with_skill vs without_skill, grades assertions via `agents/grader.md`, and emits `benchmark.json` + an HTML viewer. The user tried it for GAD's workflow methodology and got inconsistent results (4/8 tasks, twice). Diagnosis: it appears to test tool calls, not workflow adherence. GAD's evaluation question is different â€” we care whether a skill changes agent behavior on a concrete task (e.g., "did the agent actually follow the session-discipline checklist"), not whether a tool was called. Policy: GAD builds its own per-skill eval harness using the agentskills.io evals/evals.json + with_skill/without_skill + benchmark.json format (so our skills can still be evaluated by skill-creator compatible tools if desired) but with a grading strategy tuned to workflow adherence: assertions explicitly target behavioral outcomes (file mutations, commit subjects, task-registry updates, trace events emitted) rather than tool calls. Harness is a new `gad eval skill ` subcommand that (1) reads evals/evals.json inside the skill dir, (2) spawns two clean-context subagent runs per test case (with_skill + without_skill) via the existing `gad worktree` mechanism, (3) captures trace events from our v4 schema (gad-50) as the observable signal, (4) grades assertions by querying trace events + file mutations + commit log instead of LLM self-report, (5) emits a benchmark.json compatible with the agentskills.io format but populated from deterministic trace evidence.
decision.impact: (1) New task queued: build `gad eval skill ` CLI subcommand. High priority â€” this is how every GAD skill graduates from experimental to canonical per gad-86. (2) Every skill in .agents/skills/ gets an evals/evals.json starter template. First candidates: create-skill (easiest to test â€” "did the agent author a SKILL.md with valid frontmatter?"), session-discipline (testable via: did the agent run gad snapshot, did it update TASK-REGISTRY.xml, did it commit with a task-id prefix?), portfolio-sync (testable via: did the agent run build-site-data.mjs, did it regenerate the generated TS, did the git commit subject match the portfolio-sync convention?). (3) The harness becomes the concrete answer to programmatic-eval GAP G2 (skill-trigger coverage) AND G11 (skill-inheritance hygiene) â€” once we can run it, we have the automated skill-effectiveness signal gad-69 has been asking for since the programmatic-eval audit. (4) This is the load-bearing piece that turns the skill-count policy (gad-81) into an enforceable system: prune by graduation status, merge experimental duplicates via merge-skill, require benchmarks for any skill that wants to be canonical.

decision.title: New eval domain â€” GUI-for-requirements-and-harness (skill-evaluation-app)
decision.summary: User idea 2026-04-09: "lets make a GUI with requirements for it and put it a part of our rounds to test our theories. which I still see an emergent page, but not a hypothesis page with graphs and etc detailing it out visually." Two nested ideas here: (A) add more visualizations to /hypotheses, (B) create a NEW eval project where the agent's task is to build a browser-based GUI for authoring requirements and running the per-skill eval harness. The latter is a new eval domain â€” distinct from escape-the-dungeon (game dev) and gad-explainer-video (video composition). Testing the same hypotheses (freedom / CSH / emergent-evolution) on a THIRD task domain: front-end application development with a defined functional spec. If the freedom hypothesis still holds on GUI development, the hypothesis generalizes beyond creative implementation. If the compound-skills hypothesis still holds, the skill evolution effect is task-domain-independent. This becomes a major signal for generalization, which per the SKEPTIC cross-cutting critique #5 is the single biggest methodology hole in the project. The GUI itself, once built, also becomes OUR tool â€” we author requirements in it, we run evals through it, we view benchmarks through it. Dogfood opportunity: eval project that, when finished, is part of the eval framework. Scaffolding queued as task 22-51. Rubric dimensions proposed: ui_usability (0.30), requirements_ergonomics (0.20), harness_integration (0.20), visualization_quality (0.15), stability (0.15).
decision.impact: (1) New eval project scaffolded at evals/skill-evaluation-app/ this turn with gad.json + REQUIREMENTS.md. (2) The project has the same three-condition structure as escape-the-dungeon: skill-evaluation-app (GAD framework), skill-evaluation-app-bare (no framework), skill-evaluation-app-emergent (inherits skills from prior runs). Follow-up task: create the bare + emergent siblings. (3) Round 5+ includes this eval domain as the generalization test â€” specifically, once escape-the-dungeon v5 runs AND gad-explainer-video runs, the third track activates. (4) Round planning gets more complex â€” /roadmap page needs to render multiple eval domains per round, not just escape-the-dungeon. (5) /hypotheses gets concrete test-domain chips under each hypothesis card showing which domains have tested it. (6) The freedom hypothesis evidence now has a clearly-defined falsification condition: if bare fails on skill-evaluation-app or gad-explainer-video while GAD succeeds, freedom is falsified for those domains.

decision.title: Adopt agentskills.io standard â€” .agents/skills/ convention + cross-client interoperability
decision.summary: The Agent Skills standard at agentskills.io defines a client-agnostic format and discovery convention for skills: every skill is a folder containing a SKILL.md with YAML frontmatter (name + description required) and a markdown body. Progressive disclosure is a three-tier load strategy â€” catalog (name+description only, ~50-100 tokens/skill, loaded at session start), instructions (full SKILL.md body, loaded on activation), resources (scripts/references/assets, on-demand). The standard's discovery convention for cross-client interoperability is `<project>/.agents/skills/` and `~/.agents/skills/` â€” widely adopted so skills installed by one compliant client become visible to any other. GAD currently uses `skills/` at the repo root, which is neither the Claude-Code native path (`.claude/skills/`) nor the cross-client convention (`.agents/skills/`). This decision adopts agentskills.io as a co-equal canonical reference alongside the Anthropic skills guide (gad-70), commits us to the `.agents/skills/` convention for installable skills, and mandates citation of both sources on every skill-related page on the site. Does NOT replace our existing `skills/` workspace directory â€” workspace skills remain at that location for in-repo use â€” but new installable skills go to `.agents/skills/` and `install.js` is extended to copy both when supported by the target runtime.
decision.impact: (1) MIGRATION COMPLETE 2026-04-09: moved `skills/` â†’ `.agents/skills/` via git mv preserving all 28 skills (27 human-authored + 1 emergent). Prebuild SKILLS_DIR updated to read from the new canonical path. Site page file references bumped to `.agents/skills/...`. (2) New `.planning/docs/SKILL-FINDABILITY-2026-04-09.md` captures the full findability investigation. (3) The SKEPTIC cross-cutting critique about "one-person research framework" gains an orthogonal defense: we align with an open standard, so our skill format is auditable against external convention. (4) STILL PENDING on task 22-46: install.js must be extended to copy `.agents/skills/` contents (minus emergent) to user workspaces when `gad install` runs. (5) Task 22-47 queued: cite agentskills.io + Anthropic PDF on every skill-related site page via the new /standards page. (6) Emergent skill lives at `.agents/skills/emergent/session-discipline/SKILL.md` â€” the subdirectory structure is the signal that marks it emergent, mirrored by the `origin: emergent` frontmatter flag.

decision.title: Skill count policy â€” hard cap 3-7 active per task, uniqueness enforced, collision detection required
decision.summary: Per the ChatGPT analysis the user shared (2026-04-09) and the agentskills.io progressive disclosure architecture, more skills does NOT equal more capability. The failure modes are: (1) context window saturation when many skills are injected or considered, (2) skill selection ambiguity when descriptions overlap, (3) latent prompt interference even when only one skill is used, (4) retrieval noise (for embedding-based lookup â€” not applicable to us yet). The agentskills.io progressive-disclosure model mitigates the biggest failure mode (saturation) by loading only the catalog at startup, but collision and ambiguity are still our responsibility. Policy committed: (A) Hard cap of 3-7 actively-loaded skills per task. The agent should select deliberately and explain why each is loaded. (B) Each skill must answer "what can this do that no other skill can?" â€” if the answer is unclear, the skill is a merge candidate. (C) Skill collision detection runs at prebuild: scan every SKILL.md description for overlapping trigger patterns, emit warnings, propose merge candidates. (D) Skill effectiveness is measured per the agentskills.io methodology: with_skill vs without_skill baseline runs per skill, stored in `evals/evals.json` inside each skill directory, aggregated into a `benchmark.json` per iteration. This is the per-skill equivalent of our eval-project rubric. (E) More skills = more pruning required. Use merge-skill (when it exists per gad-73) to fuse overlapping skills rather than accumulate them.
decision.impact: (1) New task queued: scan `skills/*/SKILL.md` descriptions for overlapping trigger keywords, emit a collision report. (2) New task queued: adopt the agentskills.io eval format for per-skill evaluation â€” author `evals/evals.json` templates for the fundamental triumvirate skills once they exist. (3) The agent-authored evaluation framework for the escape-the-dungeon family remains the rubric-based GAD eval; the per-skill evaluation is the agentskills.io methodology. These are complementary, not competing. (4) Skills directory site page gains a "collision candidates" section if any are detected. (5) Data provenance surface on /data gains a new source: "skill-collision-scan".

decision.title: Eval isolation â€” worktrees must not inherit parent monorepo skills, hooks, or settings
decision.summary: User concern 2026-04-09: "a concern of mine when running the evals and rounds is that we might be polluting the experiments with our repo and monorepo installation." Valid concern. Eval runs currently live under `.claude/worktrees/agent-*/` spawned by `gad worktree`. Inside that worktree, the agent may inherit: (a) the parent repo's `.claude/settings.json` (including our trace hook handler absolute path), (b) workspace skills at `vendor/get-anything-done/skills/` (reachable via parent-directory scan or symlink), (c) GAD CLI tooling on PATH, (d) any environment variables set in the parent session. For the bare eval condition â€” which is supposed to test "no framework" â€” any of these leakages would contaminate the experiment by giving the bare agent implicit access to GAD affordances it shouldn't have. This decision commits us to eval isolation: (1) Eval worktrees run with an explicit-include list of files â€” the agent sees ONLY the eval project's requirements, the AGENTS.md for its workflow condition, and whatever skills the condition explicitly declares inherited. (2) The trace hook handler is ALLOWED (it's the instrumentation layer per gad-50 â€” not a framework affordance). (3) Everything else from the parent repo is either hidden, removed from PATH, or explicitly blocklisted. (4) An isolation audit runs before every round 5+ eval verifying no parent-repo state is reachable from inside the worktree.
decision.impact: (1) New open question captured: does Claude Code actually honor `.claude/settings.json` from parent directories inside a worktree, or only the worktree's own? Needs concrete verification. (2) Task 22-48 queued: write an isolation audit script `scripts/audit-worktree-isolation.mjs` that walks a fresh worktree and reports every file + env var + path entry the agent could see. Run it before round 5. (3) SKEPTIC gains a new cross-cutting critique: "eval isolation is unverified â€” previous eval scores may be contaminated by parent-repo state." (4) Round 5 prerequisite: pass the isolation audit OR document exactly which parent-repo affordances are allowed + why. (5) New tasks under phase 22 capturing the investigation + fix.

decision.title: find-skills scope â€” installed skills in user workspace + pointer to install sources, not a global crawl
decision.summary: Per gad-73 the fundamental triumvirate is find-skills + merge-skill + create-skill. User clarification 2026-04-09: "our find-skills should probably just be for installed skills and giving us suitable options to merge or to point us to installing some skills from skill.sh or the gad cli and we just install from the gad github repo since its public." Concrete scope for find-skills (to be built): (1) scan the USER'S current workspace at `.agents/skills/` and `.claude/skills/` (the standard-conformant convention + Claude Code's native path, per gad-80), (2) scan the USER'S global skills at `~/.agents/skills/` and `~/.claude/skills/`, (3) return a ranked list of relevant skills for the current task based on description matching, (4) when no suitable skill is found, suggest candidate skills from external sources â€” skill.sh, the GAD github repo (github.com/MagicbornStudios/get-anything-done/tree/main/skills/), and Anthropic's official examples repo (github.com/anthropics/skills) â€” with install commands. find-skills does NOT: (a) crawl the network looking for skills unsupervised, (b) auto-install anything (the user approves), (c) return skills from external sources alongside local ones without clearly labeling origin. This keeps find-skills scoped to "what do I have access to RIGHT NOW, and what should I install if I want more." The triumvirate audit task (queued from gad-73) must now include building find-skills to this spec.
decision.impact: (1) Task for triumvirate audit extended: find-skills spec is the scope defined above. create-skill and merge-skill specs still TBD. (2) When find-skills is built, it becomes the primary entry point for the skill-evolution loop. The agent runs find-skills first, then merge-skill if a suitable fundamental exists, then create-skill only if no merge candidate was found. (3) find-skills output format: structured JSON with `installed: [...]`, `suggested_from_external: [...]` with origin labels, so the user can see which are trusted (installed locally) vs unverified (external suggestions). (4) External-source integration with github.com/MagicbornStudios/get-anything-done is trivial â€” same-owner public repo, no auth needed. skill.sh and anthropics/skills integration is a follow-up.

decision.title: Hook-level introspection during evals â€” what "thoughts" can we actually capture?
decision.summary: User question 2026-04-09: "what are some of the thoughts that claude or an agent is having that we can hook in to and analyze during evaluations." Concrete answer based on what Claude Code's hook system exposes today: (1) PreToolUse + PostToolUse hooks fire on every tool call with the tool name, arguments, and result. Captured by our trace v4 handler (gad-58, gad-50). This gives us observable ACTION, not thought. (2) Extended thinking blocks â€” when the Anthropic API emits `thinking` content blocks in a response, these appear in Claude Code's session.jsonl but are NOT exposed via hooks today. They are the closest thing to "agent thought" we can get. Currently we can access them POST-HOC by reading session.jsonl but cannot act on them during a run. (3) Message text between tool calls â€” the agent's natural-language output when explaining or planning. Captured in session.jsonl, also not hooked. (4) Permission/approval events â€” when the agent requests a permission, we see the request and the user's response. Captured via hooks. What we CAN do today: read session.jsonl after a run completes and extract the thinking blocks + message text as an offline enrichment of TRACE.json, annotate each thinking block with the tool uses that followed it, and use that as a signal for "agent reasoning quality" during a run. What we CANNOT do today: interrupt a run based on thinking content, steer it via the thinking stream, or measure thought quality in real time. The gap is Claude Code's hook surface, not the framework's.
decision.impact: (1) New programmatic-eval gap captured in GAPS.md as G13: extract thinking blocks and message text from session.jsonl post-hoc and attach to TRACE.json under a new `derived.reasoning_trace` field. Offline enrichment â€” no new hooks required. Feasibility: high. Severity: medium (it's agent self-narrative which is suspect, but having it is better than not having it). (2) New open question: is there a programmatic signal we can extract from thinking blocks that correlates with run quality? Candidate: ratio of "exploration" thoughts to "execution" thoughts, thought-to-tool-use interval, thought length variance. Needs exploratory data analysis once we have the extraction pipeline. (3) Does NOT commit us to interrupting runs mid-flight based on thinking â€” that would require hooking the message stream, which Claude Code doesn't expose today.

decision.title: Dogfood failure observed â€” session 2026-04-09 built GAD without using GAD
decision.summary: During the 2026-04-09 IA refactor session, Claude Code (the agent building the project) shipped 20+ surfaces (pages, components, prebuild extensions, decisions) without following GAD's own workflow. Specifically: (1) did not run `gad snapshot` between turns â€” ran it once early in the session and never rehydrated, (2) did not check TASK-REGISTRY.xml for matching planned tasks despite 22-20 /gad page / 22-21 /methodology / 22-23 /projects / 22-24 interactive charts / 22-25 nav update / 22-31 Remotion scaffolding all being PLANNED in the registry for exactly the work that was then done ad-hoc, (3) did not invoke a single one of the 26 skills shipping in `skills/`, most notably `portfolio-sync` whose entire purpose is the workflow the agent executed manually, (4) did not spawn any subagents (gad-planner, gad-phase-researcher, gad-codebase-mapper all existed and were relevant), (5) wrote SKEPTIC.md into `.planning/` without checking whether that location is in `gad snapshot`'s output surface â€” it is not, so the doc is invisible to future agent sessions unless a human points at it, (6) used Claude Code's session-scoped TaskCreate tool instead of TASK-REGISTRY.xml, creating 40+ ephemeral tasks that vanish when the session ends. This is the most meta failure possible: the agent testing whether framework discipline helps agents was not using the framework. Full writeup in `.planning/SESSION-REFLECTION-2026-04-09.md`. THIS IS A FINDING AGAINST THE COMPOUND-SKILLS HYPOTHESIS as currently framed: skills do not compound if the agent doesn't invoke them, and nothing in the framework currently forces invocation.
decision.impact: Immediate fixes this session: (1) new `skills/session-discipline/SKILL.md` codifying the hard-start checklist every agent turn must follow (snapshot â†’ check registry â†’ scan skills â†’ adopt or create task â†’ work â†’ write skills for non-obvious patterns â†’ update registry â†’ update STATE â†’ commit with task id â†’ push), (2) SKEPTIC.md gets a new dogfood-failure section, (3) new task queued: extend `gad snapshot` to list `.planning/*.md` ad-hoc docs so future sessions see ASSUMPTIONS/GAPS/SKEPTIC/SESSION-REFLECTION automatically, (4) new task queued: retroactively update TASK-REGISTRY.xml marking 22-20/22-21/22-23/22-24/22-25 as done where the work actually shipped. Longer-term: the framework needs self-enforcement â€” hooks at session boundaries, skill-trigger coverage checks (GAPS G2) that fail when a tool-use pattern matches a skill description but no skill invocation event fires, commit-message linting that rejects commits without a task id. Until that happens, agents will pattern-match around the framework and the research data we collect will be contaminated by "agent did not actually use framework."

decision.title: task_pressure operationalized â€” programmatic, structural, Shannon-inspired
decision.summary: Decision gad-75 introduced "pressure" as a first-class dimension but left the formula open. User answered the open question on 2026-04-09: pressure is computed PROGRAMMATICALLY from REQUIREMENTS.xml STRUCTURE, with no human override, no `` block, and no subjective scoring (ambiguity, failure cost, etc. are dropped as sub-dimensions). The formulation borrows the Shannon-entropy intuition without computing entropy directly: "pressure â‰ˆ the number of decisions/questions required to satisfy the system." Each requirement is a question, each gate is a resolved state, more requirements and more cross-cuts mean more decisions. Concrete inputs: (a) R = count of `<requirement>` elements (including inside `<addendum>`), (b) G = count of `<gate>` elements, each weighted 2x because a gate groups multiple decisions, (c) C = count of `amends` attribute values across requirements (cross-cut dependencies), (d) W = word-count proxy for raw scope. Formula: `task_pressure_raw = R + 2*G + C` and `task_pressure_score = log2(task_pressure_raw + 1) / log2(MAX_EXPECTED + 1)` normalized to 0-1. Stored in TRACE.json per run (not a rubric dimension â€” Q2 answered A, metadata only). Critical naming clarification: this is `task_pressure` (structural, programmatic) and must NOT be conflated with `game_pressure` (the player-experience concept of stamina / resource exhaustion / time pressure in the escape-the-dungeon game). They are separate systems. The UI may display either as "pressure" but the internal names stay distinct.
decision.impact: (1) Prebuild gains a `computeTaskPressure(xml)` function called once per REQUIREMENTS.xml version, emitting per-version scores to `TASK_PRESSURE` in eval-data.generated.ts. (2) /roadmap's `PRESSURE_BY_ROUND` hand-typed constant is replaced by computed values, with a data-provenance note pointing at the formula. (3) Per-run pages gain a `task_pressure` badge showing the requirements-version pressure that run was operating under. (4) Cross-round comparison pages can now normalize: "agent X scored 0.8 at task_pressure 0.42." (5) gad-75 supersedes: the 5-subdimension framing is replaced by the structural 3-input formula (R/G/C with W as a secondary proxy). gad-75's conceptual framing remains valid as motivation; gad-79 is the operational definition. (6) The formula is intentionally simple and transparent so it can be audited and disputed. Open question pressure-score-formula is RESOLVED by this decision. Open question pressure-as-rubric-vs-metadata is RESOLVED as metadata-only. (7) game_pressure remains as the in-game design concept referenced in REQUIREMENTS addendum R-v5 items and the /requirements page rendering; no overlap with task_pressure.

decision.title: Pressure is a first-class eval dimension â€” the measurable axis connecting game-feel, requirements, and skill evolution
decision.summary: Introduced during the IA planning conversation on 2026-04-09: "Pressure" becomes a first-class evaluation dimension alongside quality, iteration count, and composite score. Working definition: pressure = the constraint intensity applied to the agent during an eval, composed of five sub-dimensions â€” (1) requirement complexity (moving parts, cross-system interactions, logic depth), (2) ambiguity (clarity, missing info, interpretation required), (3) constraint density (hard rules, architecture constraints, stack lock-in), (4) iteration budget (tool-use ceiling, retry passes, rate/529 limits), (5) failure cost (gate vs scored, visible vs hidden, cascading vs isolated). Pressure is what the requirements have been implicitly encoding, what the game design has been trying to deliver to players, and what the eval framework has been partially measuring without naming. Requirement versions implicitly ARE pressure tiers â€” v1 was low pressure, v5 is high pressure, and rounds ARE pressure transitions. Promoting pressure to a named variable lets us normalize cross-round comparisons and ask "how did agent X perform under pressure tier Y" instead of just "what score did X get". This is distinct from the rubric (which measures result quality) â€” pressure is metadata about the test conditions. Pressure ALSO connects the game-feel thread (players feel pressure as challenge, ingenuity is pressure on the player) to the eval-rigor thread, which is the project's strongest differentiator. The landing page value-prop reframes around "evaluating and evolving agents through real tasks, measurable pressure, and iteration" (gad-76).
decision.impact: (1) Pressure score formula is a new research task â€” open question opened in data/open-questions.json. (2) REQUIREMENTS.xml templates gain an optional `` block per version capturing the five sub-dimensions as author-rated scores; this is self-rated metadata initially, refined later with programmatic extraction. (3) Per-run page gains a pressure tier badge next to the composite score. (4) /roadmap timeline renders pressure progression across rounds as a new axis. (5) A future /pressure page (queued, not yet built) will let users see agent-performance-vs-pressure as a scatter. (6) Findings writeups must name the pressure tier they're evaluating under. (7) This decision is the biggest single-session conceptual shift the project has made; it reframes the landing page entirely and becomes a major signal of research credibility versus "just another agent framework" positioning.

decision.title: Target users + value-prop framing â€” researchers primary, indie devs experiential, pressure as the hook
decision.summary: Formal statement of who the site is for and what it says. **Primary user: coding-agent researchers** (target A) â€” people evaluating skill frameworks, hypothesis quality, and measurement rigor. They read findings, click through to DECISIONS.xml on GitHub, scrutinize the rubric construction. They tolerate dense content and reject marketing copy. **Secondary user: indie devs curious about skill evolution** (target C) â€” they land via playable demos, stay for the research transparency, need jargon wrapped in tooltips. **Not the audience (yet):** enterprise teams evaluating for procurement, framework authors looking to fork GAD wholesale. **Value proposition working line:** *"A system for evaluating and evolving agents through real tasks, measurable pressure, and iteration."* This combines three load-bearing ideas: evaluating agents on real tasks (not benchmarks), measurable pressure (gad-75), and iteration (compound-skills hypothesis). **Explicitly NOT the value prop:** "Use GAD to ship software faster." The freedom hypothesis (gad-36) shows bare agents outperform framework agents on greenfield creative implementation â€” GAD's value is the measurement layer and task-management substrate, not shipping speed.
decision.impact: (1) Landing page rewrite lands this session: hero headline uses the pressure framing, primary CTA is Play (B), above-the-fold priority stack is Play â†’ Methodology â†’ Findings â†’ Hypothesis â†’ Fork. (2) Copy across all pages must assume primary user A (no oversimplifying, no marketing), but must wrap jargon in Term tooltips for secondary user C. (3) Everything we build must serve user A or user C â€” if it serves neither, it's out of scope for now. (4) The open question gad-value-prop-landing-page-framing is RESOLVED by this decision + the landing rewrite that ships alongside it. (5) ASSUMPTIONS.md captures full details of both users + what we're NOT building for them.

decision.title: Contribution flow is human-first â€” snapshot is agent-facing, not human-facing
decision.summary: Important clarification the user surfaced during the IA planning conversation: `gad snapshot` is a command for AGENTS to rehydrate context after compaction â€” it produces no useful output for a human reading it. A human contribution flow must NOT include running snapshot as a step. The correct human flow is: (1) clone the repo, (2) npm install (or pnpm/yarn), (3) open the repo in Claude Code (or Cursor/Codex/other agent runtime), (4) the GAD skills are already installed in the repo so the agent has them available, (5) the human talks conversationally to the agent about running an eval or introducing a new hypothesis. The human never reads STATE.xml; they read the site's /findings, /decisions, /questions pages. The agent reads STATE.xml on their behalf when they ask it to. **Contribution pages must explicitly separate the human path from the agent path** so users don't follow agent-facing instructions and get confused by the output. Decision also commits us to TESTING the contribution flow on a fresh clone in a new repo before publishing the /contribute page, to verify skills are actually present and usable without manual setup steps we've forgotten to document.
decision.impact: (1) /contribute page ships with two clearly separated sections: "Human workflow" and "What the agent does behind the scenes". (2) The human workflow is bare bones: clone â†’ install â†’ open in Claude â†’ talk. No snapshot, no XML editing, no planning loop explanation. (3) The agent workflow section is a short explainer so humans know the difference but aren't expected to follow it. (4) Before shipping /contribute, we run a fresh-clone test in a new repo to verify: (a) skills copy into .claude/, (b) the agent can snapshot on first run, (c) a conversational request like "run the escape-the-dungeon-bare eval" actually works. (5) No PR template, no eval submission rubric, no contribution guidelines doc yet â€” those are process overhead we don't need until we have contributors. (6) CONTRIBUTING.md in the repo root is queued as a follow-up after the fresh-clone test succeeds.

decision.title: Rounds framework â€” requirements-versioned, progressive complexity, hypothesis revisits allowed
decision.summary: Formal statement of the rounds framework the project has been operating under informally. (1) A round is DEFINED by a requirements-document version. New requirements version = new round. This is the unit of "what are we testing against." (2) Multiple eval versions can ship inside a single round â€” round 4 has etd v9, etd v10, bare v5, emergent v4 (4 builds across 3 projects) and all belong to round 4 because they all target requirements v4. Within-round versions exist because different agents and workflows produce different builds against the same requirements, and because we retry runs that were rate-limited or api-interrupted. (3) Rounds are progressively MORE complex â€” requirements expand monotonically from v1â†’v2â†’...â†’v5. Each round's requirements include or supersede the previous. No requirements are silently dropped; deferrals are explicit in the version history. (4) Earlier rounds CAN be re-evaluated with NEW hypotheses. Not every build in an earlier round can be trusted for every hypothesis â€” the measurement frameworks, metrics, and harnesses at round 2 were weaker than at round 4. But earlier rounds still produce data useful for hypotheses that weren't the primary test at the time. (5) A new hypothesis can START a new round against the current requirements version OR an earlier one. Example: "does codex-cli build similarly to claude-code given the same v4 requirements?" is a valid new round-against-v4 producing a new line of data without requiring a v6. (6) COMPLEXITY LADDER as a research tool: different requirements versions represent different complexity levels; a hypothesis might only be answerable at a specific level. A runtime comparison (claude vs codex) might work best at v3 where the requirements are simpler and the comparison is cleaner.
decision.impact: Site must surface: (a) per-eval-project list of rounds the project has participated in with which requirements version was targeted, (b) per-round list of every eval version that ran, (c) per-run display of both the requirements version targeted AND a link to the previous round's requirements for context. REQUIREMENTS-VERSIONS.md is the authoritative ladder. New eval runs MUST pick a requirements_version that exists in REQUIREMENTS-VERSIONS.md; if a new version is needed the version must be added before the run. The `/roadmap` timeline page (task 88) renders this framework as the narrative of the project â€” what requirements version was tested when, what shipped, what got deferred. Earlier-round revisits (e.g. running codex against v3) are first-class and must not be labelled as "outdated" â€” they're new data points with different complexity.

decision.title: Fundamental skills triumvirate â€” create-skill / merge-skill / find-skills as GAD's foundational set
decision.summary: User vision: GAD provides three fundamental skills that together enable the emergent-evolution hypothesis (gad-68) to work in practice. (1) **find-skills** â€” locate a trusted fundamental skill from the GAD ecosystem. Examples of fundamentals: scientific-method, debug, write-tests, profile-performance, investigate-failure. Inspired by https://skills.sh/vercel-labs/skills/find-skills but scoped to the GAD trusted pool initially (expanding later). (2) **merge-skill** â€” fuse a fundamental into a more project-attuned skill. Example: merge scientific-method into kaplay-rune-spellcrafting-debug, producing scientific-method-for-kaplay-rune-spellcrafting â€” a project-tailored skill with the fundamental's rigor baked in. (3) **create-skill** â€” author genuinely new skills when no merge candidate exists. This triumvirate is the META LAYER of the in-game rune/spell merging mechanic the evals are producing: the agent's skill library evolves via the same combine-fundamentals pattern the game teaches players. This is not coincidence â€” it's the most visible instance of the craftsman metaphor (gad-68) working at two levels simultaneously. Goal: build up a library of skills with fundamental expert practices tailored in, across many greenfield prototypes + brownfield applications. Security posture: the bigger vision is to "provide security for skills, prove skills effectiveness and workflows with builds" â€” a trust model where merging a fundamental into a tailored skill carries the fundamental's provenance forward.
decision.impact: Audit task queued (task 103 follow-up): walk skills/ and identify which of the triumvirate already exist. create-skill likely exists. merge-skill and find-skills may not. Build whichever is missing. Mark the triumvirate as `category: foundational` in frontmatter so they're visually distinguished on the site's skills directory (task 85). The emergent eval template must bootstrap-inherit all three fundamentals so every emergent run has the tools to find+merge+create from day one. This also answers the CSH hygiene question from gad-65: a healthy emergent run should show evidence of find-skills lookups, merge-skill invocations authoring tailored variants, and create-skill usage only when merging failed. Tracked via hook events per GAPS G2 and G11.

decision.title: GAD's actual value proposition â€” task management at scale in-repo + skill evaluation, NOT "ship software faster"
decision.summary: User articulation: "the main value I am seeing with GAD is not software implementation fully as products. but definitely a good task management at scale all while in repo. get-anything-done's repo and the site/data is all in repo and we encourage others to clone and contribute any experiments and builds they want." Formal statement of GAD's value proposition, anchored by months of eval data: (1) **Durable task management at scale with everything in-repo.** `.planning/` XML + `data/` JSON + `evals/` artifacts + `site/` generated TS â€” no external database, no external service, no SaaS lock-in. Fork the repo, run `gad snapshot`, contribute. (2) **Scientific framework for proving skill effectiveness and workflow discipline.** The eval framework is the load-bearing feature: it tests whether skills, frameworks, and workflows actually produce better work using structured experiments (rounds, rubrics, gates, derived metrics). (3) **Skill security posture** (longer-term ambition per gad-73) â€” trust model, provenance, evaluation of effectiveness. Explicitly NOT GAD's primary value: "use GAD to ship your production software faster." The freedom hypothesis (gad-36) already showed bare agents can outperform on creative implementation. GAD may produce disciplined garbage for greenfield game dev while being excellent for task-heavy coordination, planning, and evaluation work. The site's framing must reflect this honestly.
decision.impact: Landing page rewrite (queued): hero copy stops implying "use GAD to build things" and leads with "understand whether agents are actually building things well, and keep everything in the repo." Open question gad-value-prop-landing-page-framing captures the specific rewrite pass. /methodology page must reference this decision so first-time visitors understand the research framing. Future eval categories (brownfield, content-pack injection per gad-66, codex-vs-claude comparison per task 89) all serve the value-prop of scientific skill/workflow evaluation, not of software shipping speed.

decision.title: data/ JSON pseudo-database for hand-curated site content
decision.summary: User proposal: "we might want to have a pseudo database that is json some json that gets stored in the repo and we use that to collect all the static data we need for the site and operate like it is a database, just in repo as dont expect customers and sensitive information at all." Formalize the pattern: `vendor/get-anything-done/data/*.json` files are the authoritative source of hand-curated content for the site. The prebuild script (`site/scripts/build-site-data.mjs`) reads them at build time and emits typed TypeScript via `site/lib/*.generated.ts`. The site never runs queries at request time â€” every page is SSG, every data file is read once at prebuild, types are statically exported. This is distinct from `.planning/` XML (which is agent-authored process state like phase/task/decision) and from `evals///TRACE.json` (which is eval-run output). `data/` is specifically for curated knowledge that doesn't belong in either of those two places: open questions for the public, observed bugs across eval runs, glossary terms, user-workflow assumptions, content packs extracted for gad-66, human-review tracking metadata, etc. Security posture is explicit: this is a static site with no customers, no authentication, no PII, no secrets. JSON-in-git is appropriate because we have no need for ACID, transactions, or concurrent writers â€” one human edits files in a branch, the prebuild regenerates, the site ships. If we ever need any of those properties (we don't expect to), the migration path is clear: JSON schemas become table definitions, the prebuild becomes a seed script.
decision.impact: (1) Create `vendor/get-anything-done/data/` with an initial set of files: `open-questions.json`, `bugs.json`, `glossary.json`, `assumptions.json`. (2) Extend `build-site-data.mjs` with a new section that reads each data file, validates shape, and writes corresponding TS exports. (3) New site routes backed by these files: `/questions` (open-questions.json), eventually `/glossary`, `/assumptions`. (4) Every file gets a top-level `_schema` field documenting its shape + a `_last_updated` field maintained by hand. (5) When a new hand-curated data type is needed, the pattern is: add `data/<name>.json` â†’ add a reader to `build-site-data.mjs` â†’ add a typed export to `lib/<name>.generated.ts` â†’ build a consuming page. No new runtime services, no servers, no API routes.

decision.title: Emergent-evolution working hypothesis â€” skill-evolution + emergent projects + tailored merging
decision.summary: Synthesis of the freedom hypothesis (gad-36), compound-skills hypothesis (gad-65), and the consistent GAD-framework hiccups observed across rounds 1-4 into a single working hypothesis. Statement: "A coding agent given blank planning artifacts, requirements, a task, and the ability to (a) create new skills, (b) merge similar skills, (c) find related skills to help, plus GAD-provided foundational skills to merge against, will produce better work than framework-constrained or bare conditions over time â€” because projects are themselves emergent, and skills must be tailored to the specific project continually and merged with similar foundational skills to maintain consistency." The craftsman/lifter metaphor: a master lifter varies exercise and lowers weight to stay sharp, the craftsman returns to fundamentals to maintain form â€” depending on the goal, the agent should tailor to the project while rehearsing fundamentals. This aligns with what the original Ralph Wiggum loop creator and the repomirror creators have written about as well. The GAD framework's role is NOT to prescribe process â€” it's to provide the foundational skill pool to merge against and an evolution substrate for project-tailored skills to form on top. This hypothesis is the research goal of the emergent eval condition; freedom hypothesis tests bare vs framework on a single round while this hypothesis tests whether GAD-with-evolution compounds over many rounds into project mastery. Distinct from vanilla compound-skills: this adds the merging + foundational-rehearsal dimensions and explicitly names GAD's role as a skill pool rather than a process prescription.
decision.impact: Round 5+ emergent eval condition must include: (1) a foundational skill pool inherited from GAD that the agent can merge against, (2) a skill-merge tool the agent can invoke (e.g. `gad skill merge `), (3) a way to mark a skill as "project-tailored" vs "foundational rehearsal" in frontmatter, (4) the Nyquist audit for each round checks whether merging happened and whether project-tailored skills compound. Site must carry this framing on the landing page and /gad route as the first-class hypothesis, replacing or supplementing the freedom-hypothesis framing. Also: projects themselves are emergent â€” acknowledge that requirements evolve as we observe what the evolution produces (v5 addendum was born from playtest, not upfront design).

decision.title: Programmatic evaluation is the priority â€” minimize agent-self-report dependency
decision.summary: User directive: "I don't want to be too dependent on the agents following guides/tasks to evaluate agents following guides/tasks." Evaluation framework must prioritize objective data produced by tooling, hooks, tracing, servers, parsing, and automation over agent self-reports, human judgment, or "did the agent follow the rubric" checks. Every new eval metric must answer "can this be collected programmatically?" before "how do we score it?" â€” and if programmatic collection is feasible, it takes priority. Human review stays for quality dimensions that genuinely need human judgment (is the UI enjoyable, does the game feel right) but everything mechanically checkable (did the skill actually load, did the gate pass, did the tool use mix look right, did the agent merge skills when it should have) must be captured automatically. This is the strongest framing of gad-61 to date.
decision.impact: New ongoing research task: audit every current eval data field for "programmatic vs human-reported" and build a gaps list. Candidates to automate immediately: skill-triggering coverage (did the skill load on the right tool_use patterns), functional gate checks (playwright smoke tests for G1-G4), tool-use discipline signals (over/under-triggering relative to skill descriptions), sub-agent spawn counts, commit rhythm, plan-adherence delta, deviation count, build+test pass/fail. Deliverable: `.planning/GAPS.md` with each gap ranked by eval impact and which phase picks it up. Every new eval metric PR must include a line stating whether the metric is programmatic or human-reported.

decision.title: Anthropic skills guide is the canonical reference for skill quality
decision.summary: The Anthropic "Complete Guide to Building Skills for Claude" (resources.anthropic.com/hubfs/The-Complete-Guide-to-Building-Skill-for-Claude.pdf) is adopted as the canonical reference for what a good skill looks like and how to test one. Three testing layers from the guide are named: (1) triggering tests â€” does the skill load on relevant / paraphrased / negative queries, (2) functional tests â€” valid outputs, API calls succeed, error handling works, edge cases covered, (3) performance comparison â€” proves the skill improves results vs. baseline using defined success criteria. Three skill categories from the guide are adopted as metadata on our authored skills: doc/asset creation, workflow automation, MCP enhancement. Iteration signals (undertriggering, overtriggering, execution issues) become named failure modes in our skill failure-mode taxonomy. Every skill we author or evolve must be auditable against this guide, and /methodology + /rubric + a new /skills-guide page must cite the PDF so users can follow our lineage to Anthropic's own guidance.
decision.impact: (1) skills/create-skill.md body must reference the guide and add a "category" frontmatter field (doc-creation|workflow|mcp-enhancement). (2) A /skills-guide route on the site embeds the three testing layers with examples and links to the PDF. (3) Every /skills/[id] detail page shows which of the three categories the skill belongs to and whether it has triggering/functional/performance-comparison tests. (4) The emergent eval's skill_inheritance_effectiveness rubric dimension is extended to check whether newly-authored skills follow the guide's description/trigger format. (5) Continual review cadence: each round's findings writeup must answer "did we drift from the Anthropic guide since the last round?"

decision.title: GAD is the universal planning framework â€” agent-agnostic, not Claude Code-specific
decision.summary: GAD (get-anything-done) is the single planning framework for all projects in this repo. Skills are methodology documents (SKILL.md) stored in vendor/get-anything-done/skills/ â€” they must work with any AI coding assistant, not just Claude Code. Claude Code-specific install paths (~/.claude/) are implementation details, not the canonical source.
decision.impact: All projects register under GAD via planning-config.toml. rp-* skills are deprecated; gad:* skills are canonical. No new framework-specific skill systems are added.

decision.title: Evals capture actual content, not just byte counts â€” token counts are the primary efficiency metric
decision.summary: Every eval RUN.md must contain verbatim CLI and raw workflow outputs, a unit-by-unit fidelity table, token counts (chars/4), arithmetic showing all formulas applied, and an agent simulation proving the next-action decision is identical with and without the CLI. Byte counts alone are insufficient because they cannot reveal whether information is actually readable or complete.
decision.impact: All future eval runs follow the scientific format defined in vendor/get-anything-done/evals/DEFINITIONS.md. v3 is the first run that meets this standard.

decision.title: Context = session-variable information units; static docs are exempt from CLI coverage
decision.summary: Context for agent resumption is defined as the 10 session-variable information units (U1â€“U10) in DEFINITIONS.md. Static docs (AGENTS.md style guide, planning model reference tables, done-task history) do not constitute context loss if absent from CLI output â€” they are read once and cached. Context loss occurs only when a unit's absence would cause an agent to make a different next-action decision.
decision.impact: Completeness scoring is now honest: units present at reduced fidelity (Truncated/Approximated) score 0.5, not 1.0. v3 completeness = 0.85, lower than v2's inflated 0.95.

decision.title: All session intents, decisions, and guidance must be captured in planning docs each session
decision.summary: Every working session that produces new decisions, requirements, or guidance must update DECISIONS.xml and the relevant planning docs before the session ends. Chat context is ephemeral â€” planning docs are the durable record. The GAD planning loop is the mechanism; this decision makes the capture requirement explicit.
decision.impact: Agents starting a new session get current decisions from the planning docs, not from reconstructing chat history. Drift between chat intent and planning state is prevented.

decision.title: repo-planner is officially a GAD project; rp-* skills are deprecated
decision.summary: vendor/repo-planner is registered as a GAD root (id=repo-planner) in planning-config.toml. It has a GAD-format .planning/ directory. Its skills (rp-*) are deprecated stubs pointing to gad:* equivalents. The manuscript skill content migrated to vendor/get-anything-done/skills/gad-manuscript/SKILL.md. The portfolio docs remain as the docs sink.
decision.impact: gad ls shows both global and repo-planner. gad state, phases, and tasks all work for repo-planner. No new rp-* skills should be created.

decision.title: GAD CLI is format-agnostic: XML, MD, MDX all valid in .planning/
decision.summary: Sub-repos and .planning/ directories may use XML, MD, or MDX. The GAD CLI must parse all three transparently. The planning sink normalizes to MDX only. compile() converts any format to MDX; decompile() restores the original format. Coding agents and the CLI treat methodology as the constant â€” format is an implementation detail.
decision.impact: gad sink validate checks parseability and round-trip fidelity, not format compliance. Any new project may choose its format. XML is preferred when both exist.

decision.title: gad-manuscript is a self-contained domain translator repo
decision.summary: gad-manuscript is a standalone repo that defines the domain translator pattern â€” adapts GAD base methodology to book/fiction creation. It owns its own SKILL.md files, evals, and planning docs while referencing gad:* base skills. The magicborn in-world lore content in the docs sink is produced by gad-manuscript skills.
decision.impact: Domain translator pattern is defined. Other verticals (product, design) follow the same pattern: standalone repo, own skills, own evals, references gad:* base. magicborn docs.project has content-skill = gad-manuscript.

decision.title: GAD v1.0.0 defining characteristic: skills-first with evaluation
decision.summary: Pre-1.0 (GSD/ralph-wiggum loop) was workflow-heavy â€” agents followed long workflow docs. v1.0.0 is skills-first: every skill has an agent-agnostic SKILL.md definition, is evaluated for output quality, and is versioned with the methodology. The CLI provides context efficiency. Evals are scientific. Traces measure what agents retrieve. Domain translators extend GAD to verticals.
decision.impact: All new skills must have SKILL.md in vendor/get-anything-done/skills/. Skills are evaluated via the eval framework. Methodology version is tracked in package.json gadMethodologyVersion field.

decision.title: gad context inlines file contents by default; --refs is the lightweight mode
decision.summary: The `gad context` command inlines all planning file contents directly into its output by default. This gives agents a single command to get full context without reading files separately. The `--refs` flag restores the lightweight behavior (file paths only). This improves the completeness score on the cli-efficiency eval.
decision.impact: Agents must use `gad context --refs` when they only want file paths. `gad context` without flags is verbose â€” suitable for session start, not for routine orientation checks.

decision.title: gad snapshot is the one-shot full project context command
decision.summary: `gad snapshot` inlines every planning file in a project's .planning/ directory (excluding archive/, sessions/, node_modules/). Priority order: STATE, ROADMAP, REQUIREMENTS, DECISIONS, TASK-REGISTRY, then alphabetical. This is the canonical command for handing an agent a complete cold-start context pack. It differs from `gad context` â€” context gives session-aware minimal refs, snapshot gives everything.
decision.impact: Agents starting a fresh session on an unfamiliar project use `gad snapshot --projectid `. `gad context` is for ongoing work within a session.

decision.title: gad sink is the canonical docs sync command
decision.summary: `gad sink` owns the four-direction docs sync surface: status (diff), compile (planningâ†’docs), sync (compile all, non-destructive), decompile (ensure dirs, create stubs), validate (mapping health). Sink is manual by default â€” no auto-compile on file change. Human-authored sink files (no generated: true frontmatter) are never overwritten.
decision.impact: New code uses `gad sink compile` or `gad sink sync`. sink status shows: ok | stale | missing | human-authored. All 7 registered roots compile to the sink.

decision.title: Missing-arg UX: print available choices and rerun hint, never a blind error
decision.summary: All GAD CLI commands that require a --projectid or --id argument must, when absent, print the list of valid choices and a ready-to-run rerun hint on stderr before exiting 1. Blind citty "Missing required argument" errors are banned â€” required: true is replaced with our own check.
decision.impact: Any future command added to gad.cjs that takes a required project/session/id arg must follow this pattern.

decision.title: TRACE.json is the machine-readable sidecar for every eval run
decision.summary: Every eval run directory must contain a TRACE.json alongside RUN.md and SCORE.md. TRACE.json records: workflow (A/B/both), per-command token counts and unit fidelity, unit_coverage map (U1â€“U12 at Full/Referenced/Truncated/Approximated/Absent), and computed totals. Agents write it using `gad eval trace write` or inline during a run.
decision.impact: Any new eval run must produce a TRACE.json. The diff command surfaces regressions and improvements per information unit. The report command shows persistent problem units across the full run history.

decision.title: gad state and gad phases default to session project when a session is active
decision.summary: When no --projectid flag is given, `gad state`, `gad phases`, `gad decisions`, `gad requirements`, `gad errors`, `gad blockers`, and `gad refs` call getActiveSessionProjectId() and scope output to the active session's project. Explicit --projectid overrides. --all bypasses session scope entirely and shows all roots. gad ls always shows all roots unscoped.
decision.impact: Agents working in a session see only their project by default. Multi-project output via --all or when no session exists. token_reduction improved from 0.874 (v5) to 0.940 (v6).

decision.title: portfolio-bare eval uses real agent sessions, not a harness â€” context_mode is self-declared
decision.summary: The portfolio-bare eval is a real agent session in a template directory (evals/portfolio-bare/template/) containing only planning docs and requirements â€” no app code. The agent opens the template in Claude Code or Codex, runs `gad session new [--fresh]` to declare context mode, plans phases from requirements, executes tasks, and writes a trace at the end. No API keys, no execSync harness.
decision.impact: The eval IS real agent work. context_delta emerges from comparing runs across context modes over time. A task that only appears in loaded runs is one the agent misses cold â€” the most actionable finding.

decision.title: GAD does not support subagents until they are formally evaluated
decision.summary: Spawning fresh agent instances (subagents via the Agent tool in Claude Code, or equivalent in other CLIs) is not part of the GAD workflow. Subagents start cold with no conversation history, burn context re-deriving state from files, introduce coordination overhead, and have uncharacterized failure modes. Until a formal GAD eval measures subagent utility against inline work, they are not a recommended tool. All work happens in a single agent session using the GAD planning loop.
decision.impact: No GAD skill should instruct an agent to spawn subagents. The Agent tool is not referenced in any SKILL.md. When context limits are approached, the correct response is to finish the current task, update planning docs, commit, and start a fresh session â€” not to spawn a subagent mid-task.
decision.revision-path: The subagent-utility eval (evals/subagent-utility/) defines the criteria for revising this decision. gad-16 may be updated to allow category-specific subagent use if eval results meet the thresholds defined in that eval's REQUIREMENTS.md.

decision.title: Context exhaustion: work through auto-compact by default, suggest restart at clean boundaries when economically motivated
decision.summary:
**Default behavior:** never stop work mid-task, never ask the user to restart, work through Claude Code's auto-compact event. After compaction, run `gad snapshot --projectid <id>` to re-hydrate and continue. The planning docs (STATE.xml next-action, TASK-REGISTRY.xml, DECISIONS.xml) are what make this possible â€” they are the durable state, not the conversation history.
**Refinement (2026-04-13):** The original "never suggest restart" rule is too absolute. There are real costs to letting a session grow indefinitely, and there are clean moments where a restart is the right call. The agent should *loosely* suggest restarts when the economics favor it.
**Cost shape of a long session:** 1. Every turn re-sends the entire conversation history as input tokens. Turn 100 pays for turns 1-99 again. Cost per turn grows linearly with session length. 2. Anthropic prompt cache has a 5-minute TTL. Idle gaps cold-start the cache and force a full re-read at full price. 3. As context fills, the model attends to more irrelevant history. Quality on the *current* task degrades even though the old turns are technically still there. 4. Context window is finite. Hitting the wall mid-task forces an unplanned auto-compact that is lossier than a planned restart.
**When a restart suggestion is appropriate:** - A phase or large task just landed and its decisions are now captured in planning docs (rehydration story is good) - The next chunk of work is clearly scoped and self-contained (no dangling in-head state) - Topic is switching (e.g., "we're done with phase 43, now let's look at phase 44 marketplace design") - The agent's context is past ~60-70% and the next task is independent - Auto-compact is imminent â€” a planned restart now beats a forced compaction mid-task
**When a restart is NOT appropriate:** - Mid-task with unwritten state in the agent's head - During an investigation where the trail of "what we ruled out" carries weight - When the next thing depends on subtle context from the last few turns - Any time the user is in flow and a restart would interrupt their thinking
**How to suggest:** loosely, with one-line reasoning ("phase done, context ~70%, next chunk is mechanical and rehydratable from snapshot â€” restart is reasonable here, or I can keep going"). The user decides. Never push, never restart unilaterally. The default still leans toward continuing â€” restart is the exception, not the norm.
decision.impact: Agents work through auto-compact by default and never restart unilaterally. They MAY suggest a restart loosely at clean planning boundaries when context is filling and the next chunk has a good rehydration story via `gad snapshot`. The user always decides. gad-04 (decisions in planning docs) is still what makes both auto-compact and planned restarts work â€” without durable docs, no rehydration story exists at all.

decision.title: The GAD loop is simple: snapshot â†’ work â†’ update docs â†’ commit
decision.summary:
The canonical GAD loop for any agent, any coding tool: 1. `gad snapshot --projectid <id>` â€” one command, full context. No reading 15 files manually. 2. Pick one task from TASK-REGISTRY.xml (status=planned). 3. Implement it. 4. Update TASK-REGISTRY.xml (mark done), STATE.xml (next-action), DECISIONS.xml (if new decisions). 5. Commit.
That's it. AGENTS.md files in each project reference this loop but do NOT redefine it. The vendor/get-anything-done/ project is the canonical source. Root AGENTS.md and .planning/AGENTS.md are consumers that point here, not parallel definitions.
Skills are methodology documents (SKILL.md) â€” agents read and follow them. They are not CLI commands or executable plugins. The GAD CLI provides context efficiency. Skills provide methodology.
decision.impact: Root AGENTS.md mandatory read order must be simplified. Agents should NOT read 15 files on startup â€” gad snapshot exists for this. AGENTS.md files across vendor projects reference the GAD loop, they don't each define their own version. No memory files, no shadow task lists â€” TASK-REGISTRY.xml and STATE.xml are the only task tracking.

decision.title: Eval agents must be bootstrapped with GAD context â€” not just prompted
decision.summary: The escape-the-dungeon eval (v2) showed that prompting an agent to "use gad snapshot" and "log traces" doesn't work â€” the agent ignored the CLI entirely, read requirements, and coded directly. Eval agents need: (1) GAD CLI available and working in the worktree, (2) AGENTS.md in the eval template that enforces the loop, (3) snapshot output injected into the agent's initial prompt (not "run this command"), (4) hooks or structured checkpoints that enforce task-level tracking. The agent must be inside the loop from the start, not asked to enter it.
decision.impact: Eval templates must include an AGENTS.md with the GAD loop. The eval-run skill must inject snapshot context into the agent prompt. The agent's working environment must have gad CLI accessible. Honor-system tracing is insufficient â€” tracing must be automated or enforced via checkpoints.

decision.title: Trace logging must be automatic â€” hooks or checkpoint-based, not manual log-cmd calls
decision.summary: Manual trace logging (gad eval trace log-cmd/log-skill) requires the agent to remember to call them. The v2 eval proved this doesn't happen â€” the agent backfilled all 8 skill triggers in 15 seconds at the end. Tracing should be: (1) automatic via hooks that detect gad CLI calls and skill invocations, or (2) checkpoint-based where the agent must produce a trace entry to unlock the next phase, or (3) post-hoc by parsing git history and planning doc diffs to reconstruct what happened. Option 3 is the most reliable since it doesn't depend on agent cooperation.
decision.impact: The eval-run workflow should reconstruct traces from git log + planning doc state changes, not rely on the agent to self-report. gad eval trace reconstruct --project <name> should parse git history to build TRACE.json after the fact.

decision.title: Greenfield eval projects must auto-scaffold CONVENTIONS.md after phase 01
decision.summary: The escape-the-dungeon agent created 13 source files with patterns (scene structure, content pack format, state management) but documented none of them. A follow-up agent would start cold with no conventions. After the first implementation phase, GAD should prompt or auto-generate a CONVENTIONS.md from the code patterns established â€” file structure, naming, import patterns, content pack format, scene registration pattern.
