decision.impact: Add a post-phase-01 checkpoint to eval-run and execute-phase that generates CONVENTIONS.md from the codebase. The gad:map-codebase skill already does this â€” it should be triggered automatically after the first implementation phase of any greenfield project.

decision.title: Snapshot includes project-scoped conventions with global fallback
decision.summary: gad snapshot includes a CONVENTIONS section: first checks project-level CONVENTIONS.md in .planning/, then extracts conventions/style sections from the project's AGENTS.md, then falls back to global root AGENTS.md conventions. Conventions grow per-project as code/content/patterns evolve â€” refactoring introduces new conventions, and the snapshot surfaces them to agents. Eval runs are expected to produce findings that tighten workflows, create new skills, and refine snapshot content.
decision.impact: Every project can have its own CONVENTIONS.md. The snapshot surfaces the right conventions for the project being worked on. After eval runs, review outputs and create/update conventions based on what worked and what didn't.

decision.title: All GAD CLI calls and agent tool uses are logged to JSONL for eval tracing
decision.summary: Every gad CLI invocation logs to .planning/.gad-log/ (JSONL, one file per day): timestamp, command, args, duration, exit code. A PostToolUse hook (tool-trace.js) logs every agent tool call (Bash, Read, Write, Edit, Agent, Skill, Grep, Glob) to the same directory. During eval runs, GAD_LOG_DIR routes logs to the eval run directory for permanent storage. `gad log show` displays both log types interleaved with filters (--filter cli|tool|gad|skill|agent). `gad eval trace from-log` builds TRACE.json from actual logs (source: call-log) instead of git reconstruction. This is the definitive, numerical, measurable observability layer.
decision.impact: Evals produce real call logs, not reconstructed approximations. Agents that spawn subagents, trigger skills, or call gad CLI commands are all traced. Log data enables: per-tool-call token estimates, skill trigger verification, gad CLI usage patterns, and cross-eval comparison on actual behavior.

decision.title: Real-time GAD tooling: core first, transports second
decision.summary: The live developer loop (watch planning files, re-run refs verify, surface results quickly) is implemented once as a small core: debounced filesystem watch, call into existing verify code paths, emit structured events (JSON lines or NDJSON) for consumers. CLI (`gad dev` / `gad watch planning`) is the first transport. An MCP server, if added, is a thin adapter over the same core â€” not a separate implementation. Primary user is humans and local tooling (terminal, scripts, CI); agent use via MCP is optional and must not block shipping the core. IDE/editor-specific integrations (VS Code tasks, problem matchers, extensions) are out of scope until explicitly re-scoped â€” not required for the core.
decision.impact: New watch/MCP code should live next to existing `gad refs` / verify libraries. No required network service for v1. MCP must not duplicate business logic.

decision.title: Tooling and MCP evals are automated, fixture-based, and comparable across versions
decision.summary: Evaluating watch/dev and MCP differs from implementation evals (agents building projects). Tooling evals use `eval_type` of `tooling` or `mcp`, a fixed `scenario` id pointing at an in-repo fixture, and an automated harness (Node script or `gad eval tooling run`) that starts the watcher or MCP client, performs scripted actions, and asserts ground truth (missing paths, exit codes) plus latency metrics. No LLM in the loop. Each run writes `TRACE.json` with `trace_schema_version` so new granular fields (p50/p95, event counts, feature_flags) can be added without invalidating old runs. Compare runs with the same scenario id to measure improvement per feature or gad version.
decision.impact: New eval projects under `evals/` for tooling/mcp. CI can run them on every PR touching gad.cjs or verify libs. Feature flags or gad version are recorded in the trace for attribution.

decision.title: Granular eval metrics: versioned schema, summary in TRACE, detail in optional metrics.json
decision.summary: Automation must not depend on regex-parsing human-oriented `SCORE.md`. Implementation evals gain `scores.json` (or equivalent) alongside `SCORE.md` with stable numeric fields. Tooling evals store a summary composite (`tooling_composite` or nested `scores`) in `TRACE.json` for gates and trends; high-cardinality data (per-invocation latencies, histogram buckets, full event traces) go in `metrics.json` or retained JSONL only when needed. Bump `trace_schema_version` when the summary shape changes. `gad eval report` / `diff` read structured JSON, not markdown, for machine comparisons.
decision.impact: score-generator and eval scores command updated to emit and read JSON. Report tables can aggregate tooling and implementation evals. Raw logs remain optional debug artifacts.

decision.title: All skills and agents must bootstrap via gad snapshot, not manual file reads
decision.summary: Skills were hardcoding "read STATE.md, ROADMAP.md, DECISIONS.md, REQUIREMENTS.md" as Step 1 â€” 4+ file reads that the gad snapshot command already provides in one low-token output. Skills must use `gad snapshot --projectid <id>` as their bootstrap step. Individual CLI commands (gad tasks, gad decisions, gad phases) are available for drilling into detail. Skills must also be format-agnostic (XML or MD) per gad-06 â€” never hardcode .md or .xml extensions when referencing planning files. CLAUDE.md enforces this at the instance level: every fresh session runs gad snapshot first.
decision.impact: All core skills updated: execute-phase, plan-phase, check-todos, quick. CLAUDE.md created as hard startup requirement. Subagent prompts should include the project ID so agents can run gad snapshot. Deprecated GSD compatibility notices removed from all skills (gad-19 already killed GSD).

decision.title: GSD is dead â€” GAD and RepoPlanner only
decision.summary: All GSD (get-shit-done) skills, hooks, commands, and references are removed from the repo. GAD is the planning framework. RepoPlanner provides the planning snapshot format that GAD consumes. No GSD commands in .claude/commands/, no GSD hooks in .claude/hooks/, no GSD references in settings.json or AGENTS.md. The context monitor and statusline hooks are useful infrastructure but must be rebranded for GAD â€” no npm checks for get-shit-done-cc.
decision.impact: Delete .claude/commands/gsd/ entirely. Remove or rebrand .claude/hooks/gsd-* files. Clean settings.json. Remove GSD references from root AGENTS.md. Keep context monitor and statusline functionality but strip GSD branding.

decision.title: Process metrics do not guarantee output quality â€” human review is the ground truth
decision.summary: GAD v7 scored 0.81 discipline and 1.0 planning quality but only 0.30 human review (broken game loop). Bare v2 scored 0.0 discipline but 0.50 human review (most playable game). Auto-composite can be misleading â€” the 15% human_review weight is insufficient. For implementation evals that produce user-facing output, human review should be weighted higher or serve as a gate.
decision.impact: Re-evaluate composite weights for game evals. Consider human_review as a multiplier rather than an additive dimension. A game that scores 0.0 human review should never have a composite above 0.50 regardless of process metrics.

decision.title: Eval requirements must be versioned and findings documented per experiment
decision.summary: Requirements changed twice in one session (original 12 criteria â†’ gate criteria + vertical slice). Each change invalidates comparison with previous runs. Requirements must carry a version tag, and FINDINGS docs must reference which requirement version was used. Scientific rigor requires reproducibility.
decision.impact: Add version field to REQUIREMENTS.xml. FINDINGS docs reference requirement version. TRACE.json includes requirements_version field.

decision.title: v4 game requirements reframed: pressure over features
decision.summary: Round 3 findings exposed that v3 was a features checklist, not a game design. Players could beat floors with default spells, forge was optional, no encounter tested adaptation. v4 flips this: every mechanic must create pressure that rewards ingenuity. Core principle: "baseline starter abilities must not be sufficient to comfortably complete a full floor without adaptation or enhancement." Specific additions: floors contain 5-8 authored rooms + boss gate; each floor must introduce a mechanical constraint that default spells can't brute-force (elemental resistance, DoT requirement, reflection, etc.); forge gate requires at least one per-floor encounter that significantly favors crafted/adapted spells; pressure mechanics required from at least 2 of 4 categories (resource, enemy counterplay, encounter design, build specialization); new ingenuity/adaptation scoring dimension measures whether players needed to craft/adapt.
decision.impact: v4 REQUIREMENTS.xml rewritten with gates reframed around friction, not feature checklists. Authored-only (no procedural generation). Room types: Combat, Elite, Forge, Rest, Event. Decay and deep evolution trees deferred to v5. Starter spells explicitly insufficient. New scored dimension: ingenuity_score = f(crafted_spells_used, spell_diversity, adaptation_required_by_encounters).

decision.title: Skills (physical actions) are a scored criterion, not a gate, in v4
decision.summary: Skills system (physical non-spell actions that evolve via own XP, can be supercharged with mana crystals) was considered for gate status but deferred. Forge is already a major gate and requiring both would cause broad-but-shallow implementations. v4 requires only that combat supports at least one non-spell action category (basic attack or skill). Full skill system with evolution and mana-crystal overcharge is scored, not required.
decision.impact: v4 gate: combat has at least one non-spell action category. v4 scored: distinct skill system, mana overcharge, DoT/stun/bleed effects. Prevents the forge gate from eating all implementation budget.

decision.title: Sprite sourcing is a workflow requirement with coherent fallback allowed
decision.summary: v4 requires agents to attempt asset sourcing first through a defined search workflow: lightweight installable icon packs, web-searched free/open-license sprites, or common art sources. Fallback to generated/programmatic placeholders is allowed only if sourcing genuinely fails or integration cost becomes disproportionate â€” but the fallback must still be visually coherent. Ugly primitive-only presentation counts against the UI quality gate regardless of sourcing attempts. Requires creating a gad:find-sprites skill (or equivalent workflow doc) that agents can reference.
decision.impact: Add a sprite-sourcing workflow skill agents can reference. UI gate stays strict â€” sourcing failure is not an excuse to ship ugly output. Authored asset sources (Pokemon-style open sprites, game-icons.github.io, etc.) preferred over procedural.

decision.title: "Traits" in UI, narrativeStats in code â€” split terminology
decision.summary: Player-facing UI shows narrative/progression stats as "Traits". Internal code/data can continue using narrativeStats as the technical term. Both must be documented with a mapping. Prevents churn of a full taxonomy rename while giving players the more legible label.
decision.impact: UI label: Traits. Code identifier: narrativeStats. Docs note the mapping once. Applies to all eval templates and game builds from v4 forward.

decision.title: Emergent AGENTS.md stops mandating specific artifacts â€” agents define their own
decision.summary: Round 3 emergent AGENTS.md prescribed ARCHITECTURE.md, CHANGELOG.md, specific directory layouts. That defeats the point of the emergent experiment, which is to observe what workflow artifacts an AI-decided workflow produces. v4 emergent AGENTS.md only mandates: (1) put all workflow artifacts in game/.planning/, (2) reference the create_skill skill to define new skills when patterns emerge. Everything else â€” what files exist, what formats, how tasks are tracked â€” is decided by the agent itself. Same applies to bare eval.
decision.impact: emergent/template/AGENTS.md rewritten to be minimal. Bare template similar. Both reference a new create_skill skill that documents how to create reusable skills. This is the only skill bare starts with; emergent starts with create_skill + inherited skills from previous runs.

decision.title: Skills evolution flow: greenfield emergent â†’ brownfield emergent â†’ greenfield (installed back)
decision.summary: The experimental model for emergent workflows: (1) greenfield emergent creates initial skills from scratch (only has create_skill skill); (2) brownfield emergent inherits those skills + creates new ones as it extends existing code; (3) when proven, those skills can be "installed back" into the next greenfield emergent run as its inheritance. This creates a feedback loop where AI-decided workflows evolve across rounds and environments. GAD represents what humans have decided is good; bare and emergent represent AI-first AI-decided workflows for achieving the desired human output. The comparison is human-defined methodology vs AI-emergent methodology.
decision.impact: Skills from each emergent run are preserved in evals/<project>/v<N>/run/.planning/skills/. When planning the next round, skills can be copied into the next template as inherited starting state. Both greenfield and brownfield can feed skills back into each other over time.

decision.title: Portfolio must host all experiment documentation before further experimentation
decision.summary: User wants to view all experiment docs on apps/portfolio: links to code, game builds, scores, process, workflows, graphs, explanations of what's being tested. The portfolio acts as the public showcase of the experiment. Before running any more eval rounds, the portfolio must contain: (1) hub page explaining the experiment, the conditions (GAD/bare/emergent), and the freedom hypothesis; (2) per-round pages with scores and links to preserved builds + run code; (3) per-eval-project pages showing version history; (4) visualizations/graphs of scores across rounds; (5) requirements version history; (6) findings docs. Everything must be viewable from either the get-anything-done repo OR the portfolio docs sink.
decision.impact: New phase 22 dedicated to portfolio experiment documentation. Blocks round 4 experimentation until complete. Uses existing apps/portfolio/content/docs/get-anything-done/ structure with new evals/ subsection. Each eval project + run has its own MDX page with links to apps/portfolio/public/evals/<project>/<version>/ for playable builds.

decision.title: GAD framework diagnosis (freedom hypothesis) is deferred â€” need more eval data first
decision.summary: The round 3 findings showing bare consistently beats GAD on creative implementation are enough to treat as a real pattern but not yet enough to commit to structural changes (GAD-lite, pivoting away from creative evals, etc.). Before making architectural decisions about GAD itself, we need: (1) round 4 greenfield with v4 (pressure-oriented) requirements to see if the pattern persists; (2) brownfield round 1 to test whether GAD's overhead pays off on codebase extension; (3) at least one more iteration of each condition to establish variance. Discussions about GAD-full vs GAD-lite vs GAD-seeded are ongoing but not action items yet.
decision.impact: Do not split GAD into variants yet. Do not abandon game evals yet. Focus round 4 on v4 requirements (pressure-oriented) with the same three conditions. Re-examine the hypothesis after round 4 and brownfield round 1 data. Keep the diagnosis thread open in DECISIONS but don't force premature conclusions.

decision.title: Eval projects categorized by mode (greenfield/brownfield) and workflow (gad/bare/emergent)
decision.summary: Evals are now categorized on two axes: eval_mode (greenfield starts from scratch, brownfield extends an existing baseline codebase) and workflow (gad uses full framework, bare creates own workflow, emergent inherits and evolves). Stored in gad.json. Shown in gad eval list. Brownfield evals reference a baseline (project+version) whose run/ becomes the starting codebase. Added three new brownfield evals: etd-brownfield-gad, etd-brownfield-bare, etd-brownfield-emergent â€” all starting from escape-the-dungeon-bare v3 as baseline, extended with v4 feature requirements (floor progression, respawning encounters, rune forge with affinity, skills system, visual upgrade).
decision.impact: Brownfield experiments test how each workflow handles long-term project evolution vs fresh starts. The freedom hypothesis (gad-36) was measured on greenfield only â€” brownfield may produce different results because GAD's overhead could pay off more when extending existing code (planning docs help track what's already there). gad worktree commands added for managing the many worktrees these experiments create. AGENTS.md for bare/emergent simplified â€” less prescriptive, lets agents figure out workflow details themselves.

decision.title: All eval workflow artifacts must live under game/.planning/ regardless of framework
decision.summary: GAD, bare, and emergent evals all must place workflow/tracking/skills/decisions/notes files under game/.planning/. Source code in src/, assets in public/, process artifacts in .planning/. This separation allows clean inspection of what methodology each eval invented without mixing it into source. The format inside .planning/ is up to the agent (XML, Markdown, whatever). Violations (files at project root or mixed into src/) are detected by gad eval preserve and logged as warnings.
decision.impact: AGENTS.md updated for all 3 eval templates with mandatory layout section. gad eval preserve detects and warns on violations. DEFINITIONS.md documents the contract. Round 3 findings: GAD runs compliant (had .planning/), bare and emergent all violated by putting WORKFLOW.md and skills/ at project root. Round 4+ will enforce.

decision.title: Eval preservation is a hard contract enforced by tests
decision.summary: Every implementation eval run MUST end with these artifacts preserved at canonical locations: (1) evals/PROJECT/vN/TRACE.json, (2) evals/PROJECT/vN/run/ with code+planning, (3) apps/portfolio/public/evals/PROJECT/vN/ with the playable build, (4) evals/PROJECT/vN/.gad-log/ with CLI logs. The gad eval preserve command copies everything after a run; gad eval verify audits compliance; tests/eval-preservation.test.cjs fails the build if any run on or after the contract cutoff is missing artifacts. Before this decision, multiple runs' outputs were lost when worktrees were cleaned up or builds were overwritten.
decision.impact: Future eval runs cannot be "forgotten" â€” the tests enforce preservation. Every agent running an eval must call gad eval preserve immediately after agent completion, before the worktree is removed. gad:eval-run and gad:eval-bootstrap skills updated with mandatory preservation step. DEFINITIONS.md documents the contract at the top.

decision.title: Freedom hypothesis: GAD constraint may correlate inversely with creative output quality
decision.summary: Across 3 rounds of escape-the-dungeon evals, Bare (no framework) consistently outperformed GAD (full framework) on human review. Round 3: Bare v3 = 0.70, Emergent v2 = 0.50, GAD v8 = 0.20. GAD has never exceeded 0.30 across 4 attempts; Bare is monotonically improving (0.10 â†’ 0.50 â†’ 0.70). GAD's planning overhead may consume tokens that could go to implementation and testing. The process is followed but the product fails. Status: PRELIMINARY â€” single-run variance is high, needs more data. Confound: rate limits may have hit GAD harder because it was mid-planning.
decision.impact: (1) Do not abandon GAD â€” this may be specific to greenfield game implementation. (2) Create a GAD-lite mode for comparison. (3) Add context-resumption eval where GAD should show its advantages. (4) Re-examine GAD's value proposition: is it optimizing for traceability/handoff rather than creative speed? (5) The game eval may be the wrong benchmark for GAD's core strengths.

decision.title: Eval game builds must be preserved per-version, never overwritten
decision.summary: Previously, gad eval open copied builds to apps/portfolio/public/evals/PROJECT/index.html, overwriting previous runs. This destroyed experimental history. Now all builds are preserved at apps/portfolio/public/evals/PROJECT/VERSION/index.html. The gad eval open command checks version-specific paths first.
decision.impact: All 8 escape-the-dungeon builds now preserved (v5-v8, bare v1-v3, emergent v1-v2). Experiments can be compared across time. Showcase path is apps/portfolio/public/evals/PROJECT/VERSION/ for every run.

decision.title: UI quality is a gate criterion at full presentation level
decision.summary: The build must include icons, visually differentiated room presentation, styled controls/panels, and entity visual representation. Easily installable icon/sprite packs, web-searched assets, or generated placeholders are acceptable when setup cost is low. Raw ASCII/text-only UI fails the gate. Geometric fallback tiles are last resort, not primary path.
decision.impact: All game eval REQUIREMENTS.xml updated to v3 with gate G3 (ui-quality). Agents must source visual assets as part of implementation. Asset sourcing can be a skill.

decision.title: Spell crafting is a gate criterion â€” loot/rewards do not satisfy it
decision.summary: The player must intentionally combine runes/components through a forge/crafting interface and produce at least one new spell outcome. Pre-equipped spells or spell rewards from NPCs/loot do not count. Minimum: 3-5 runes, 3+ valid combinations, crafted spell usable in encounters.
decision.impact: Gate G2 added to REQUIREMENTS.xml v3. No previous run has satisfied this â€” all runs will need to implement a forge system for the first time.

decision.title: Human review weight raised to 0.30 with low-score caps
decision.summary: human_review weight increased from 0.15 to 0.30 in composite formula. Added hard caps: if human_review < 0.20, composite capped at 0.40; if < 0.10, capped at 0.25. This prevents technically compliant but broken/unusable output from ranking highly. Process metrics cannot override a bad user experience.
decision.impact: Composite formula updated in DEFINITIONS.md and gad.cjs. All existing auto_composite scores are now provisional â€” human review is required for the real score.

decision.title: Bare eval condition is a live contender, not a deprecated baseline
decision.summary: Bare v2 scored 0.50 human review (most playable game) versus GAD v7 at 0.30. The bare condition responds well when informed by prior failure signals, making it a valid forward-looking experimental condition. All three conditions (GAD, Bare, Emergent) remain in active testing with equal priority.
decision.impact: Do not deprioritize or drop the bare condition. Continue iterating on all three. The bare condition's strength is adaptation from failure feedback â€” test that hypothesis further.

decision.title: Inherited skills must capture failure modes and fixes, not just patterns
decision.summary: Emergent v1 inherited 4 skills from bare v1 but still failed (KAPLAY styled text error). The skills documented code patterns but not failure modes. Bare v2 (told about v1 failure, no inherited skills) succeeded because it had to solve the problem itself. Skills that only document "how I did it" without "what went wrong and how I fixed it" are insufficient for cross-run learning.
decision.impact: Skill format should include: pattern, when-to-use, failure-modes, and known-fixes sections. Previous-workflow.md should include specific error messages and resolutions, not just "it didn't work."

decision.title: Bare and Emergent bootstrap skill set is a pinned invariant: create-skill + find-sprites
decision.summary: Any bare or emergent greenfield eval template MUST include skills/create-skill.md and skills/find-sprites.md in its template/skills/ directory before the eval runs. These two are the minimum bootstrap methodology an agent needs to author its own workflow (create-skill) and source coherent visual assets (find-sprites). Emergent projects may inherit ADDITIONAL skills from previous runs, but the two-skill floor is non-negotiable. Regressing the bootstrap set without a corresponding requirements update invalidates cross-round comparisons for bare/emergent.
decision.impact: portfolio-sync skill enforces this during site sync; any regression becomes visible because the /projects/[id] page shows the bootstrap set per eval and the catalog inheritance map flags the mismatch. If we intentionally change the bootstrap set (e.g. add a third skill), we bump the requirements version and document the change in REQUIREMENTS-VERSIONS.md.

decision.title: Trace schema v4: every tool use, skill invocation, and subagent spawn must be captured
decision.summary: Today's TRACE.json has a bare numeric skill_accuracy field for most runs (e.g. escape-the-dungeon v8 = 0.17 with no breakdown of which of the 6 expected triggers fired vs missed). This means we can't interpret scores across workflows. Trace schema v4 will capture: (1) every tool_use with timestamp + name + outcome; (2) every skill invocation with trigger context (user phrase, auto-detected, explicit slash command); (3) every subagent spawn with inputs + outputs + duration; (4) every file mutation with path + size delta. Schema versioned so older traces still parse; rendering on the site degrades gracefully for pre-v4 runs.
decision.impact: Phase 25 is the backing work. Per-run pages on the site will show a "tracing gap" callout for runs whose TRACE.json is pre-v4 and surface the full breakdown for v4+. Framework versioning (also phase 25) is a prerequisite because we need to know which trace schema the run was recorded against.

decision.title: Framework is versioned on branches so evals can pin + re-run across versions
decision.summary: main = current. Frozen snapshots live on version/v1.1, version/v1.2, etc. When we ship a framework change that could affect eval scores (new skill, changed command, different subagent wiring), we branch, tag, and update a FRAMEWORK_VERSION constant used by `gad version`. Every TRACE.json stamps the commit it ran against so we can distinguish "the agent did better" from "the framework changed." Re-running past evals against the current framework becomes a scheduled activity, and score drift between framework versions becomes visible.
decision.impact: Phase 25 builds the branching strategy, `gad version` command, and the stamping. skills/framework-upgrade/SKILL.md is the methodology agents follow when proposing a framework change. Without this, cross-round comparisons that span framework updates are dishonest â€” we're comparing two different systems.

decision.title: Remotion is how we ship video explainers â€” player, not compiler
decision.summary: Remotion's @remotion/player is a React component that renders video compositions live in the browser without requiring MP4 export. Free for teams â‰¤3 and reuses existing React components (SiteHeader, score cards, SVG charts) as video frames. No separate asset pipeline â€” video IS composable React. Phase 26 scopes what videos we want (GAD loop walkthrough, v8 failure dissection with tracing overlay, freedom hypothesis explainer, bare-vs-emergent emergence comparison) and plans the Player integration before implementing. MP4 export only if we need social-media distribution later; primary mode is interactive browser playback.
decision.impact: Phase 26 is a discuss-first phase. No Remotion code ships until we've scoped the video list and confirmed the React reuse pattern. Decision captured now so future sessions don't have to rederive "why not Adobe After Effects" â€” the answer is "because our React components are already the scenes we want to show."

decision.title: Trace schema v4 requires hook-aware agents â€” other coding agents are explicitly unsupported for tracing
decision.summary: Phase 25's trace schema v4 captures every tool_use, skill invocation with trigger context, and subagent spawn with inputs/outputs. The only reliable way to capture this data is from within the coding agent's runtime via hooks â€” Claude Code's PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks, Aider's Python callbacks, Continue.dev's extension API. Agents without hook runtimes (Cursor's closed-source editor, basic Codex CLI, vanilla ChatGPT) cannot produce trace v4 data and are explicitly unsupported for GAD evaluation. This isn't a limitation we're going to work around â€” attempting to reconstruct tool calls from stdout parsing or screen-scraping is lossy and fragile. We'll publish an Agent Runtimes comparison on /methodology listing which coding agents are evaluable and which aren't, and why.
decision.impact: Phase 25 eval harness wiring is Claude-Code-specific (via its hooks). Future support for other agents requires each one to expose a structured tool-use event stream. The site's /methodology page has an Agent Runtimes table explaining this; the agent runtime requirement becomes a gate criterion in the eval project gad.json so runs against unsupported agents are rejected at the eval-run step.

decision.title: Eval backfill policy: freeze old scores, stamp new runs with framework version
decision.summary: When the framework changes (new skill, new subagent, rewritten command), old eval scores are never recomputed. They're historical records, frozen at the framework commit they ran against. New runs against the updated framework get stamped with the new framework commit and become the "current" data point for cross-version comparisons. This preserves the archaeology â€” a reader can see what a v5 escape-the-dungeon run scored in April 2026 against framework v1.32.0 and trust that number. Framework drift is detected by re-running the same eval against the current framework and diffing, not by rewriting history.
decision.impact: skills/framework-upgrade/SKILL.md documents this as the invariant. The site's /methodology page explains it. Trace schema v4 adds a mandatory gad_version + framework_commit field so every run is self-describing about which framework it ran against. No backfill commands exist in the CLI; any attempt to rewrite an old TRACE.json is a bug.

decision.title: Multi-agent tracing is a conversion problem, not a reimplementation problem
decision.summary: If we ever want to evaluate Codex, Aider, or other hook-capable agents, the path is: their native session format â†’ a converter script that emits GAD trace schema v4 â†’ the same /runs/[project]/[version] page renders it. No per-agent site code. Codex's output format (Running/Ran prefixes with output blocks under ` â”” `) is parseable but stream-based â€” would need a converter that tails the session and emits trace events. Captured here so we don't go down the "let's add Codex-specific TRACE fields" path when the right answer is a converter.
decision.impact: Phase 25 ships the Claude Code converter first. Future phases can add codex-to-trace or aider-to-trace converters as needed. The trace schema itself stays agent-agnostic. Unit of comparison is "a trace", not "a trace from Claude Code specifically."

decision.title: Lineage attribution: GSD â†’ RepoPlanner â†’ GAD
decision.summary: GAD's lineage is acknowledged explicitly on the public site. Get Shit Done (gsd-build, https://github.com/gsd-build/get-shit-done) is the upstream inspiration â€” small planning loops, visible state, executable specs. RepoPlanner (b2gdevs, https://repo-planner.vercel.app/) was an earlier attempt by the same author to formalize the "Ralph Wiggum loop" (continuous agent work maintained by in-repo documents and tasks) â€” the core insight: keep docs and tasks in the repo so long development always has context and something to work on. GAD builds on both: the planning rigour of GSD, the in-repo context persistence of RepoPlanner, plus the eval framework as the thing that distinguishes us â€” we actually measure whether the loop helps. The /lineage page on the site tells this story with concrete links and explains the context rot problem each approach tries to solve.
decision.impact: /lineage page exists and links to both upstream projects. Any talk of "what GAD is" has to route through this lineage â€” we're not claiming to have invented structured agent planning, we're claiming to have taken two previous approaches and added measurement. Keeps us honest and gives visitors a starting point if they'd rather use GSD or RepoPlanner.

decision.title: Remotion parameters: 30s max, cinematic passthrough, stoppable, /videos route + inline embeds
decision.summary: Phase 26 parameters pinned: every composition is max 30 seconds, rendered as a cinematic passthrough (linear timeline, no branching), but the player must expose play/pause so viewers can stop mid-scroll. Videos live in two places: (1) inline "watch this dissection" embeds on relevant per-run pages â€” e.g. the v8 failure dissection shows up on /runs/escape-the-dungeon/v8, and (2) a dedicated /videos route that lists every composition with a search/filter so visitors can browse them standalone. All compositions reuse existing site React components (Graphs, ScoreBar, Cards, etc) as video frames â€” no separate asset pipeline. No MP4 compilation. @remotion/player handles all rendering client-side. Composition registry lives in site/remotion/Root.tsx.
decision.impact: Phase 26 is now execute-ready. No more discussion needed on length, format, playback, or placement. Next sessions can author individual video compositions against this spec and register them in the registry. Remotion Player is free for teams â‰¤3; no paid license needed for the current shape of the project.

decision.title: Skill invocation marker: hybrid file + hook-emitted event
decision.summary: The agent writes a single plain-text file at .planning/.trace-active-skill containing just the active skill id. That's the entire agent-side burden â€” one file write per skill start. The hook handler reads this file on every PreToolUse event to attribute subsequent tool uses to the active skill (adds trigger_skill field on each event) and detects content changes to emit a discrete skill_invocation event whenever the active skill transitions. Gives us both low agent burden and structured/objective data: the hook is the source of truth for the skill_invocation event stream, not the agent's self-reporting. For nested skills (skill X applies skill Y), the currently-active skill is whichever one the file last pointed to, and the skill_invocation event records the parent as whatever was active before the transition.
decision.impact: Phase 25 implements the hook logic that reads and watches .planning/.trace-active-skill. The skills/ documentation is updated to instruct agents to write the marker on skill start and clear it on skill completion â€” this is a low-burden contract that can be enforced via linting. Alternatives considered and rejected: (a) explicit bash command marker â€” more agent burden, duplicates events in the tool stream; (b) inference from tool use patterns â€” too lossy, not objective.

decision.title: Hook handler lives in the GAD repo, referenced by absolute path from user settings
decision.summary: The hook handler script lives at vendor/get-anything-done/bin/gad-trace-hook.cjs (or equivalent in the distributed GAD package). `gad install hooks` writes an absolute path reference into the user's ~/.claude/settings.json pointing at the installed GAD binary. This gives us one canonical handler per framework commit, zero per-workspace drift, updates via `gad update`. `gad uninstall hooks` is the reverse operation. Handler receives arguments via stdin per Claude Code's hook contract and appends JSONL events to <project-root>/.planning/.trace-events.jsonl (append-only, gitignored).
decision.impact: Phase 25 ships both `gad install hooks` and the handler script. Users run the install command once per workspace; subsequent framework updates pick up new handler behaviour automatically because the path reference points at the installed package. Alternative rejected: per-workspace handler written by install â€” drifts from framework version, harder to update, defeats the purpose of centralised versioning.

decision.title: Trace event output field capped at 4 KB with head/tail truncation
decision.summary: Individual event `outputs` fields in the JSONL stream are capped at 4096 bytes. When raw output exceeds the cap, it's split into 2 KB of head + 2 KB of tail joined by a "... [truncated N bytes] ..." marker, and the event is stamped with `truncated: true`. Full untruncated output remains in Claude Code's session.jsonl which is accessible on disk for any run that needs deeper inspection. The 4 KB cap keeps noisy runs (hundreds of events over a 15-minute session) under 1-2 MB of trace data, comfortably within git's working-tree range.
decision.impact: Phase 25 hook handler implements the truncation. The site's per-run page renders the head/tail split as a collapsible code block with a "view full output in session.jsonl" link. Future sub-phase can add full-output lookup if we find ourselves reaching for session.jsonl frequently.

decision.title: Parallel eval runs share a single account rate limit â€” serial execution is the default
decision.summary: Round 4 attempt #1 launched GAD + Bare + Emergent in parallel via three worktree agents. All three hit an account-level rate limit simultaneously at ~14 minutes (tool_uses 81/45/40) with the message "You've hit your limit Â· resets 12am America/Chicago". The shared bucket meant none of the three runs finished. Implication: parallel eval runs on one account are a false economy. Serial execution (one agent to completion before launching the next) is the new default. Parallel is only acceptable when we have documented per-account capacity and a pre-calculated budget for the combined workload.
decision.impact: Eval runner behaviour change: `gad eval suite` no longer implies parallel execution. Future round 4 retries run serially (one greenfield condition at a time), expected 20-30 min each, 60-90 min total wall clock. Parallel execution available as an explicit flag if/when per-run budget is known.

decision.title: Compound-Skills Hypothesis â€” emergent workflow as a skill-growth framework tailored to a specific project domain
decision.summary: The emergent eval condition is not just "inherit some skills and don't regress." It is a testable hypothesis about knowledge compounding: **as an emergent agent iterates on the same project domain across rounds, its inherited skill library should grow more specialized to that domain, and the game it produces should improve in quality and requirements-alignment per round more than bare produces by starting fresh.** The craftsman metaphor: a blacksmith who learns one skill per hour and authors one skill per hour becomes a master of the specific kind of blade they keep forging. The test signals: (1) human review score increases monotonically across emergent versions; (2) inherited skill count grows across rounds; (3) the ratio of project-specific skills to generic methodology skills trends toward specific; (4) time-to-working-game decreases across rounds despite the requirements getting stricter. Round 4 emergent v4 produced the first observed full inheritance â†’ apply â†’ evolve â†’ document cycle (authored dom-over-kaplay + pressure-forge-coupling and deprecated kaplay-scene-pattern). The next emergent run (v5) is the first real test of whether v4's learnings actually help. DISTINCT from the freedom hypothesis (bare beats GAD on creative implementation) â€” CSH is specifically about whether the emergent mechanism produces compounding returns over time, independent of the bare vs GAD framing.
decision.impact: Phase 27 rubric adds a sixth dimension for emergent runs: skill_inheritance_effectiveness (weight 0.20), which directly scores whether a run productively applied inherited skills, evolved them, and authored new skills worth inheriting. Site's /projects/escape-the-dungeon-emergent page gains a skill lineage view showing which skills each run inherited, evolved, deprecated, and authored. Findings writeups track CSH metrics separately from freedom-hypothesis metrics. Every emergent run's TRACE.json should capture inherited_skill_count, authored_skill_count, deprecated_skill_count, and (optionally) a specificity_score measuring how tailored the authored skills are to the roguelike domain vs generic workflow.

decision.title: API-interrupted runs are a separate failure category from rate-limited runs
decision.summary: Round 4's GAD v10 hit Anthropic API 529 overloaded_error twice. This is server-side transient load, not an account rate limit â€” different cause, different retry semantics, different interpretation. TRACE.json now distinguishes the two via separate flags: timing.rate_limited (account cap hit â€” gad-63 treats these as preserve-but-exclude) and timing.api_interrupted (server overload â€” ALSO preserve-but-exclude, with interruption_reason field capturing the specific HTTP error). Both flags filter runs out of cross-round quality comparisons in the site's Graphs and Results sections. The distinction matters because rate-limited runs imply budget planning issues while api-interrupted runs imply external infrastructure issues â€” researchers reading the /findings/ round writeups need to see which failure mode they're looking at.
decision.impact: site isRateLimited() helper extends to isApiInterrupted() and comparableRuns() excludes both. Per-run pages get an amber "API interrupted â€” server overload" badge distinct from the "rate limited â€” account cap" badge. TRACE.json schema allows both flags on the same run (a run could theoretically hit both).

decision.title: Rate-limited runs are preserved as data points but excluded from cross-round quality comparisons
decision.summary: TRACE.json files from rate-limited runs are still preserved (the partial work is informative about planning differential, architecture patterns, and inheritance effects) but explicitly excluded from freedom-hypothesis comparisons, Graphs scatter plots, and cross-round score aggregation. The filter key is `timing.rate_limited === true` in TRACE.json. Site rendering (Results, Graphs, /insights) should filter these out by default with an opt-in "include rate-limited" toggle for researchers. The human_review.notes field on every rate-limited TRACE carries "DO NOT include in cross-round comparisons against completed runs" as the explicit instruction.
decision.impact: Site's Results cards, Graphs scatter plot, and per-round summaries default-filter rate_limited runs. The runs still show up on /runs/[project]/[version] pages with a "rate limited" badge and a link to the partial-results finding doc. Raw data is preserved forever; comparison treatment is explicit.

decision.title: Authored-content injection experiment â€” inherit content packs alongside requirements
decision.summary: New eval condition proposal: preserve every run's authored game content (spells, runes, items, NPCs, dialogue trees, skills, encounter tables) as a portable "content pack" separate from the source code. A subsequent eval run inherits both the requirements document AND a content pack extracted from prior runs, then builds on top. This is NOT a freedom-hypothesis test â€” it explicitly scaffolds the agent with pre-authored content and measures how much more fleshed out the resulting game becomes given the same requirements. User's framing: "given some requirements and some already made content, it will build on top of that. this will just possibly make a more fleshed out game." Terminal research objective (user verbatim): "have a really complex game made with all the builds and data around it for coding agent transparency and ability." The intent is to accumulate a growing canon of authored content across rounds so that eventually the escape-the-dungeon canon has enough depth that a single agent run can produce a genuinely complex game. Distinct from the emergent workflow which inherits *skills* (meta-methodology); this inherits *content* (domain data). The two can be combined: emergent+content run inherits skills AND a content pack.
decision.impact: Requires a new extraction step in `gad eval preserve` that writes an authored-content manifest (content-pack.json + per-category json files) alongside the existing code + planning artifacts. Requires a new eval project flavor (e.g. escape-the-dungeon-inherited or a content_pack field on gad.json) that specifies which prior run's pack to inherit. Site gains a "content lineage" view per project showing which content objects originated in which run and which runs inherited them. Queued as a new phase task, not blocking round 5. Round 5 still runs on v5 requirements without content injection to keep the freedom/CSH tests clean.

decision.title: Serial eval execution is the permanent default â€” parallel requires explicit authorization
decision.summary: Reinforcement of gad-62 with a stronger default. User directive after round 4: "our tests done sequentially to use the full token/budget that we get for these evals is more than likely what we will need to do rather than run them all in parallel most of the time. we will just need to remember to eval." Policy: any agent session proposing an eval run defaults to serial execution (one condition at a time, to completion, allowing the full per-session token budget to absorb any single run). Parallel execution is an explicit exception requiring (a) a stated reason why serial is insufficient, (b) a pre-computed token budget showing parallel runs will not collide on the shared account cap, and (c) explicit user authorization in the current session. Agents MUST NOT batch-launch parallel eval worktrees as a default. "We will just need to remember to eval" is also captured: agent sessions working on eval-relevant code must proactively surface "run the eval to validate?" after significant requirements or template changes.
decision.impact: Update any documentation that implies parallel is the default (gad:eval-run skill, AGENTS.md for evals, portfolio site eval docs). `gad eval suite` docstring already clarifies this but should gain a top-of-output warning when invoked without the --parallel flag. Agents pick one eval at a time and run it to honest completion before queuing the next.

decision.title: Research priority: structured objective data production for evaluations
decision.summary: The primary goal of the eval framework is no longer "score runs" â€” it's "produce structured data that yields insights." A score is just one kind of derived data; the framework should also produce structured human review rubrics (dimensions instead of single numbers), automated gate checks (playwright tests instead of human inspection for things automatable), derived metrics (tool mix, commit rhythm, plan-adherence deltas, divergence scores), cross-run aggregation queries, and visualizations that surface patterns rather than just summaries. Phase 27 is the research methodology phase that designs all of this in parallel with phase 25's raw event capture. Future decisions about what to measure next always answer "does this yield an insight we can represent objectively?" before "how do we score it?"
decision.impact: Phase 27 opens in the roadmap as a peer of phase 25. skills/objective-eval-design/SKILL.md captures the methodology. Phase 25 is about getting the raw events; phase 27 is about what we do with them. The two phases can proceed in parallel â€” phase 27 designs against v3 trace data today and gets richer inputs when v4 lands.

decision.title: GAD self-evaluation â€” real-world brownfield data from building GAD itself
decision.summary: Building GAD + the site IS a GAD project. We have 7 GAD workspaces in this monorepo, 2320+ tool call events across 4 days, 1000+ trace events, 93 decisions, 141 done tasks. This is real brownfield data â€” not isolated game evals. We should be scoring ourselves per "round" of GAD framework development: how well did GAD help us build GAD? Per-round metrics from the trace data (tool mix, commit rhythm, context compactions, skill invocations) become the first brownfield eval that isn't escape-the-dungeon. Every GAD project in the monorepo (grime-time, repub-builder, mb-cli-framework, etc.) is potential eval data for the framework.
decision.impact: New data source for the site: real-world GAD usage metrics alongside the controlled game evals. The landing page should show both: "here's how 3 workflows compare on a game" AND "here's how GAD performs on real multi-project work." This is the most credible evidence for or against GAD because there's no cherry-picking â€” it's our actual work.

decision.title: Reverse-engineer eval: requirements quality measured by build success
decision.summary: The reverse-engineer skill produces requirements from an existing codebase. The only way to know if those requirements are good is to build from them â€” same methodology as game evals. Eval flow: reverse-engineer a project â†’ agent builds from the generated requirements â†’ score the result against the original. This is a new eval project (reverse-engineer-eval) with its own scoring rubric focused on requirements completeness and fidelity. The reverse-engineer skill should work in conjunction with map-codebase â€” map gives structural understanding, reverse-engineer distills actionable requirements. If the target has GAD planning docs, those should be consumed as additional signal.
decision.impact: New eval project: reverse-engineer-eval. New skill tooling: reverse-engineer + map-codebase integration. The eval for reverse-engineer is inherently two-phase (produce requirements, then build from them) which makes it the first eval that tests a GAD skill rather than a game implementation workflow.

decision.title: Skills that measure GAD are framework-level, not private
decision.summary: trace-analysis and self-eval are public framework skills in .agents/skills/ â€” they measure GAD itself, which is useful to any GAD user. Private project-local skills (.claude/private-skills/) are reserved for truly project-specific workflow automation that has no value outside the specific repo. Skills don't need tooling to be evaluated â€” they can be evaluated through rounds by observing whether agents use them effectively. The "skill invocation gap" in hook traces is a methodology observation, not a bug to fix.
decision.impact: trace-analysis and self-eval moved to .agents/skills/. The private tier (.claude/private-skills/) remains available but only for genuinely project-specific automation. Skill evaluation happens through rounds, not through mandated tooling.

decision.title: Remove /gad:join-discord â€” dead command
decision.summary: The /gad:join-discord command is not useful. Remove it from the help reference and skill registry.
decision.impact: One fewer command in the help output. No workflow impact.

decision.title: Skills prefer CLI commands but use good judgment â€” methodology skills are valid
decision.summary: Skills should use CLI commands when they exist â€” `gad eval setup` not manual file creation. But this is judgment, not a rule. Toolless methodology skills are perfectly valid. The goal is to pack utility into the CLI and have skills leverage it, creating a cycle: CLI grows â†’ skills chain commands â†’ overlapping skills get merged â†’ CLI absorbs proven patterns. When 3 sequential commands solve a workflow, that's a skill. But pure instructional skills (like self-eval rubrics or debugging methodology) don't need tooling to be useful or evaluable â€” we evaluate through rounds.
decision.impact: CLI and skills co-evolve. Neither is mandatory. Good judgment about when to use tooling vs methodology. Every new CLI command should be checked against existing skills for replacement opportunities.

decision.title: GAD-specific skill creator built from Anthropic's skill-creator + our artifacts/CLI
decision.summary: Install the Anthropic skill-creator (`npx skills add https://github.com/anthropics/skills --skill skill-creator`) and use it to create a GAD-specific derivative. The GAD skill creator knows our planning artifacts (STATE.xml, TASK-REGISTRY.xml, DECISIONS.xml), our CLI commands, our eval framework, and the agentskills.io conventions. It doesn't create more skills for our artifacts â€” it creates skills tailored to how we work WITH our artifacts and CLI. When 3 sequential CLI commands solve a workflow, the GAD skill creator recognizes that pattern and produces a skill that chains them. merge-skill must reference agentskills.io docs as professional guidance during merges.
decision.impact: New skill: gad-skill-creator (in .agents/skills/). Built using the Anthropic skill-creator's references and methodology. merge-skill updated to reference agentskills.io. Skills become lighter as CLI grows.

decision.title: gad data â€” CRUD subcommand for site/data/ and data/ JSON pseudo-database
decision.summary: Add `gad data list|get|set|add|remove` subcommand for CRUD operations on the JSON files in data/ (repo-level pseudo-db) and site/data/ (site-level static data). Treat it like a local database with CLI access. Examples: `gad data list` shows all collections, `gad data get hypotheses.freedom` reads a specific entry, `gad data set rounds."Round 5".status awaiting_review` updates a field. This replaces manual JSON editing and makes data operations traceable in the log.
decision.impact: New CLI subcommand. Skills and agents use `gad data` instead of reading/writing JSON directly. All data mutations are logged, making self-eval metrics more accurate.

decision.title: Skill creation immediately produces an eval project on the site
decision.summary: When a skill is created via gad:create-skill, immediately scaffold an eval project for it (`gad eval setup`) and register it on the site. GAD framework skills (skills/commands/agents related to the framework itself) must be labeled as framework-level and require formal evaluation. Non-framework skills used in real workflow still show usage data on the site from trace logs â€” the data is already being collected. The distinction: framework skills get formal controlled evals, all skills get observational usage data from real work.
decision.impact: gad:create-skill workflow now includes `gad eval setup --project ` as a mandatory step. Site shows two data types: formal eval scores (controlled) and real-workflow usage metrics (observational). Framework skills are tagged for identification.

decision.title: Computed trace metrics are long-lived â€” raw logs are capped
decision.summary: Raw trace logs (.gad-log/*.jsonl, .trace-events.jsonl) have caps (1000 events for trace, daily rotation for gad-log). But computed/mined values from those logs are long-lived static data â€” once computed, they persist in site/data/self-eval.json and don't need recomputation. The compute-self-eval pipeline runs during prebuild and produces snapshots. Key metrics to mine per computation window: tokens used per phase, tokens for the whole project thus far, number of tasks completed, pressure score (per gad-75/gad-79 definitions), skill invocation counts, framework overhead ratio, loop compliance. These are expensive to compute from raw logs so we gather everything we can during the computation window and store the results permanently.
decision.impact: self-eval.json grows over time as an append-only metrics store. Each prebuild adds a new snapshot. Raw logs can rotate freely because the computed values survive independently. The pipeline needs to capture per-phase token counts and pressure scores in addition to current metrics.

decision.title: Task-skill-agent attribution is a formal tag in GAD artifacts
decision.summary: Every task in TASK-REGISTRY.xml should tag which skill(s) and agent(s) were involved. Format: `skill="skill-name"` and `agent="agent-name"` attributes on the task element. Named agents (gad-planner, gad-verifier, etc.) use their name; unnamed/default agents use "default". Multiple skills on one task signals a merge/enhance opportunity. The AI knows during the session which skills and agents are active â€” capture it in artifacts since we can't always rely on trace logs. Standard naming convention follows existing GAD patterns: lowercase-kebab-case for skills/agents.
decision.impact: TASK-REGISTRY.xml tasks gain skill="" and agent="" attributes. Planning doc updates include attribution. Enables querying "which skills were used for which tasks" from artifacts alone without trace logs.

decision.title: Enhance workflow: review task, find repetitive commands, propose script/CLI/skill
decision.summary: Add "enhance" as a workflow alongside merge. When reviewing a completed task: (1) what repetitive commands were there? (2) should we create a script and invoke that? (3) or stack commands and keep instructions? (4) or officially add to the gad CLI because it's workflow-related? This is the feedback loop from usage to tooling. A task that used 5 sequential Bash commands is a candidate for a script. A task that chained 3 gad CLI calls is a candidate for a new composite command. Enhance doesn't replace merge â€” merge handles overlapping skills, enhance handles graduating patterns from tasks into reusable tooling.
decision.impact: New workflow step after task completion: enhance review. Feeds the CLI-grows â†’ skills-thin cycle from gad-99.

decision.title: Defer gad data CLI â€” keep it light
decision.summary: The gad data CRUD subcommand (gad-101) is deferred. Keep the CLI light. JSON files are readable and editable directly. Add data commands only when there's a proven workflow need, not speculatively.
decision.impact: gad-101 deprioritized. Focus CLI work on eval and rounds commands instead.

decision.title: gad eval --input for skill installation into eval projects
decision.summary: The eval CLI should support installing skills into eval project templates before or after runs. `gad eval run --install-skills path/to/skill [...]` installs skill(s) into the eval template, populates the data (AGENTS.md skill references, template/skills/ directory), and tracks what was installed. This enables: (1) point at any skill folder and wrap it as an eval, (2) install skills into existing eval projects that already have source from prior runs, (3) run evaluations with vs without specific skills. The CLI handles the automation of copying skills, updating AGENTS.md, and recording the installation in TRACE.json. Works with npx skills format, local paths, and GitHub URLs.
decision.impact: New eval CLI flag. Enables skill evaluation without manual template editing. Tracks skill installations as eval metadata. Prioritize before gad data CLI.

decision.title: Pipeline is a formal artifact category alongside skill, agent, eval
decision.summary: Pipeline tasks (compute-self-eval, build-site-data, etc.) are a distinct category in our artifact tracking. The table format with Type column (skill, eval, pipeline) is the standard for listing artifacts. Pipeline tasks should be tagged in TASK-REGISTRY.xml same as skills and agents. Decoupled piping with the GAD CLI is the design goal â€” pipelines are composable CLI command chains.
decision.impact: TASK-REGISTRY.xml gains type="pipeline" alongside type="skill" and type="agent". Artifact tables include Pipeline as a category.

decision.title: gad data uses lowdb with /data as the managed database
decision.summary: The gad data CLI subcommand uses lowdb (https://github.com/typicode/lowdb) with the data/ folder as the database. JSON files in data/ are both the database AND version-controlled codebase artifacts â€” lowdb reads/writes them directly. This gives us CRUD operations via CLI while keeping the data human-readable and git-tracked. Implement after eval --install-skills ships.
decision.impact: Add lowdb as a dependency. data/*.json files gain programmatic CRUD via gad data. Still human-editable and git-tracked.

decision.title: Eval domain categories: game, skill, software, business, stories
decision.summary: Eval projects are categorized by domain â€” not just mode (greenfield/brownfield) and workflow (gad/bare/emergent). Domains: game (escape-the-dungeon, tech-stack comparisons), skill (reverse-engineer, gad-skill-creator), software (business apps, CMS templates), business (productivity tools), stories (narrative content, compositions). The domain determines the build requirement â€” games need a playable build, skills need a requirements file, software needs a running app, stories need a composition. Domain is a field in gad.json.
decision.impact: gad.json gains `domain` field. gad eval list shows domain column. Site groups evals by domain. Playable archive becomes multi-domain (games + software + readmes).

decision.title: Tech stack comparison evals: same requirements, different frameworks
decision.summary: For game evals, compare tech stacks: Phaser, PixiJS, Three.js, Babylon.js alongside existing Kaplay/DOM builds. Same requirements, different stack in the template. The tech stack is metadata in gad.json (`tech_stack` field). Eval matrix: requirements with tech stack specified, requirements without tech stack (agent chooses), requirements with skills, requirements with skills + tech stack. This produces data on whether framework choice or skill availability matters more for output quality.
decision.impact: New eval projects per tech stack. gad.json gains tech_stack field. Eval comparison includes tech stack as a variable. Site shows tech stack badges on eval cards.

decision.title: gad eval inherit-skills â€” CLI command to copy emergent skills from one eval into another
decision.summary: Easy CLI command to inherit skills from a completed eval run into a new eval template. `gad eval inherit-skills --from escape-the-dungeon-bare/v5 --to escape-the-dungeon-emergent`. This is the mechanism for testing GAD+Skills (with inheritance). The command copies skills from the source run's game/.planning/skills/ into the target template, records the lineage in metadata. Distinct from --install-skills which installs framework skills â€” this inherits agent-authored runtime skills.
decision.impact: New CLI command. Enables easy emergent skill testing. Skills lineage tracked in eval metadata.

decision.title: Evals have timestamps, build requirements, and completion gates
decision.summary: Every eval run is timestamped (started, ended) in TRACE.json. An eval is only "done" when: (1) the build requirement is produced (game build, requirements file, composition, running app), (2) human review is submitted. Build requirement is defined per domain in gad.json as `build_requirement` field. For reverse-engineer, the build is the requirements file â€” but it's gated until a second eval implements those requirements and produces a working build. For video evals, the build is the Remotion composition (src is the build until mp4 export exists). Eval data includes source file sizes alongside build sizes.
decision.impact: gad.json gains build_requirement field. TRACE.json gains source_size_bytes and build_size_bytes. Eval completion is gated on build + review. Site shows both sizes.

decision.title: Human time estimation vs agent time â€” comparison metric per eval
decision.summary: Before each eval run, the agent estimates how long the requirements would take a human developer to implement (without AI tools). This goes into TRACE.json as `human_estimate_hours`. After the run, we have actual agent duration. The ratio (estimate / actual) shows the speedup factor. This is publishable data â€” "this eval's requirements would take a human ~40 hours, the agent did it in 23 minutes." Time estimation is part of the eval prompt, not a separate step.
decision.impact: TRACE.json gains human_estimate_hours. Eval prompt includes estimation instruction. Site shows speedup ratio. Powerful marketing data.

decision.title: Pressure per phase drives skill creation opportunities
decision.summary: When pressure is high on a phase (many tasks, many crosscuts per gad-75/gad-79), that signals skill creation opportunities â€” the complexity required more work, meaning patterns exist that could be captured as skills. After completing a high-pressure phase, review for: repeated commands (â†’ script/CLI), repeated patterns (â†’ skill), repeated coordination (â†’ agent). Pressure scores should be computed per phase and displayed on the site with interactive visualization. The Gantt chart idea: phases as horizontal bars, click for tasks, hover for stats (pressure, tokens, task count).
decision.impact: Pressure computed per phase in the self-eval pipeline. Site gets interactive Gantt chart component for phases and rounds. High-pressure phases flagged for skill creation review.

decision.title: Playable archive redesign for thousands of evals across domains
decision.summary: The Playable Archive needs redesign for scale â€” thousands of evaluations across game/skill/software/business/stories domains, with relationships between evals (e.g., reverse-engineer â†’ build-from-requirements). Non-GUI builds show their README. Multi-domain filtering. Tech stack badges. Source + build size display. Related eval linking. This is a Phase 28+ project.
decision.impact: Major site redesign queued. Current playable archive works for dozens of game builds but won't scale to thousands of multi-domain evals.

decision.title: Quick start guide + simplified install â€” no repo clone required
decision.summary: GAD needs a quick start guide for onboarding: install Claude Code or Codex CLI, install GAD (`npx get-anything-done`), run first eval. No repo clone required for users â€” the eval framework should work from any project. Documentation priority: quick start, eval setup guide, skill creation guide, CLI reference.
decision.impact: New docs: quick-start.md, eval-guide.md. Install flow simplified. Site gets /quickstart route.

decision.title: GAD discipline scoring for evals using the framework
decision.summary: Evals running with the GAD workflow should compute GAD discipline scores from their traces â€” same metrics as self-eval (overhead ratio, loop compliance, planning doc freshness, decision capture rate). These scores live in the eval's TRACE.json and are displayed on the run detail page. The eval's source files retain trace data for future reference. README of each eval repo should include scores when reviewed. CLI automates score injection into READMEs.
decision.impact: Eval TRACE.json gains gad_discipline section. CLI command to inject scores into README. Site displays discipline scores alongside quality scores for GAD workflow evals.

decision.title: Interactive Gantt chart for phases and rounds with hover stats
decision.summary: Replace or augment the current phase/round displays with interactive Gantt charts. Phases as horizontal bars on a timeline. Click a phase to expand tasks. Hover for stats (pressure score, token count, task count, duration). Same pattern for RoundResults â€” Gantt chart of rounds, click shows results, hover shows summary stats. Use recharts or a lightweight Gantt library compatible with the existing site stack.
decision.impact: New component: GanttChart in components/charts/. Used in both Workflow section (phases) and RoundResults section (rounds). Pressure data feeds the visualization.

decision.title: Skill provenance tags: installed, inherited, authored, 3rd-party, gad-framework
decision.summary: Skills in an eval run have provenance: (1) installed â€” put in template via --install-skills before the run, (2) inherited â€” copied from a prior run via inherit-skills, (3) authored â€” created by the agent during the run (didn't exist at start), (4) 3rd-party â€” from external sources (npx skills), (5) gad-framework â€” official GAD skills. Provenance is tracked in .installed-skills.json and diffed against the post-run skill directory to identify authored skills. The site displays provenance tags on skill badges. GAD framework skills have a formal visual treatment distinct from inherited/authored.
decision.impact: Eval TRACE.json gains skills_provenance section. Site shows provenance badges. inherit-skills gains --latest-only flag option.

decision.title: Rounds are per-project, defined by requirements version changes
decision.summary: A "round" for a project is defined when its requirements document changes version. Each eval project has its own round timeline, not a single global round number. The current global rounds (1-5) are specific to the escape-the-dungeon family. Other projects (reverse-engineer, skill-evaluation-app, etc.) have their own round 1 when they first run. Round number is derived from the requirements_version field progression in TRACE.json across runs.
decision.impact: gad rounds gains --project flag for per-project rounds. Site shows per-project round timelines. Global rounds remain as the escape-the-dungeon view for backward compatibility.

decision.title: gad self-eval CLI command for pressure and framework metrics
decision.summary: Add `gad self-eval` CLI command that runs the compute-self-eval pipeline and prints findings. Pipeline scripts should have corresponding CLI commands so agents and users can invoke them. `gad self-eval` runs compute-self-eval.mjs and outputs: pressure per phase, overhead ratio, loop compliance, high-pressure phases. This is the CLI-first principle from gad-99.
decision.impact: New CLI command. Replaces needing to run node scripts/compute-self-eval.mjs manually.

decision.title: Docs must be on the site â€” docs/ markdown files need site routes
decision.summary: Documentation in docs/ (quick-start.md, eval-guide.md) must be rendered as routes on the site, not just exist as markdown files in the repo. The site is the primary documentation surface. Docs routes: /quickstart, /eval-guide. These should be built from the markdown files during prebuild, same as other content.
decision.impact: New site routes. Build pipeline renders docs/*.md to site pages.

decision.title: Playable archive becomes a dedicated /evals page â€” catalog/marketplace pattern
decision.summary: The current playable archive on the landing page works for showing the last 5 rounds of game builds. But we need a dedicated /evals page that shows ALL evals across ALL rounds as a catalog/marketplace. Filter by domain, tech stack, round, workflow, review status. Show build size + source size. Show skill provenance. Show eval relationships (e.g., reverse-engineer â†’ build-from-requirements). Everything is free â€” no paywalling. The landing page keeps a compact "latest" view linking to the full /evals page.
decision.impact: New /evals route. Landing page playable archive becomes a compact preview. Full catalog on dedicated page.

decision.title: ID format: per-project namespace + type prefix + number
decision.summary: IDs encode project, artifact type, and number. Format: PROJECT-TYPE-NUMBER. Examples: GAD-D-119 (GAD decision 119), GAD-T-22-01 (GAD task phase 22 task 01), GAD-P-22 (GAD phase 22), ETD-T-3-01 (escape-the-dungeon task). Type codes: D=decision, T=task, P=phase, R=requirement, B=bug, S=skill. Namespace is per-project (GAD, ETD, GRIME, etc.) not always "GAD" â€” GAD is the project name, not a framework prefix. New IDs use this format; old IDs stay until a migration script handles them as a batch. On the site: each segment color-coded (namespace=accent, type=type-color, number=foreground). HoverCard on every ID shows full artifact details with segment breakdown (e.g., "22 = phase, 01 = task"). Traceability over brevity â€” full IDs everywhere, no abbreviations in cross-references.
decision.impact: New IDs in DECISIONS.xml, TASK-REGISTRY.xml use new format going forward. CLI output shows new format. Site Ref component gains HoverCard + color-coded segments. Migration script (gad migrate-ids) for batch conversion when ready.

decision.title: GAD CLI is an operational CLI â€” distinct from coding agent CLIs
decision.summary: The GAD CLI (gad) is an operational CLI for the GAD framework and ecosystem. It is NOT a coding agent CLI like Claude Code CLI or Codex CLI. Those are coding agent runtimes. GAD provides commands, agents, skills, and artifacts FOR those coding agent CLIs. The site must make this distinction clear â€” framework + catalog on the same page, with the GAD CLI described as operational tooling that agents and users invoke, not the agent itself. The /gad page skill bootstrap sets should show the various hypotheses and what artifacts/tools are available per hypothesis condition.
decision.impact: Site /gad page redesigned. Framework + catalog merged. Clear copy distinguishing operational CLI from coding agent CLIs. Build/release pipeline for the GAD CLI as a distributable.

decision.title: Official build/release pipeline for GAD CLI â€” Windows first, publish ourselves
decision.summary: Ship an official build/release pipeline for the GAD CLI. Windows first (build on this machine, publish ourselves). Mac and Linux can use GitHub workflows. Users install via npx get-anything-done or a published binary. The CLI should let users set up blank projects or eval projects. The /quickstart flow: install coding agent CLI â†’ install GAD â†’ set up project or eval â†’ run.
decision.impact: New release pipeline. Windows binary published manually. Mac/Linux via GitHub Actions. npx install flow verified end-to-end.

decision.title: /project-market â€” dedicated catalog page for all eval projects
decision.summary: Ship /project-market as the dedicated catalog page listing ALL eval projects (not just ETD + explainer). ETD and explainer are featured/canonical (pinned, badged) but not exclusive. Default filter: last 5 rounds per project (not global). Home page playable archive shrinks to a teaser linking to /project-market. Full handoff spec committed as PROJECT-MARKET-SPEC.md. Projects own rounds. Requirements change â†’ new round. In-round edits allowed until new evals branch.
decision.impact: New /project-market route. Home page lightened. All 30+ eval projects browsable with default filtering. Featured canonical projects visible.

decision.title: Rubric belongs on project detail and eval pages, not standalone /rubric
decision.summary: The /rubric page content should live on project detail pages and eval run pages where it's contextual. A standalone /rubric page may remain as a methodology reference but the primary rubric experience is per-project and per-run.
decision.impact: Rubric display on project detail + run pages. /rubric becomes secondary reference.

decision.title: Projects have evals, evals have rounds, rounds triggered by requirements changes
decision.summary: Conceptual hierarchy: Project â†’ eval projects â†’ rounds. A round is triggered when requirements change. Within a round, requirements can iterate without triggering a new round until new evals branch or the contract fundamentally changes. Per-project round windows for display filtering.
decision.impact: Data model clarification. Per-project round timelines on site. CLI round numbering per-project.

decision.title: Methodology must be tied to a buildable project with artifacts
decision.summary: Even methodology-only tracks must implement a buildable project. No catalog entry without a concrete eval project and evaluation path. If methodology produces signals (pressure, compliance, traces), artifacts must ship. GAD-on-GAD is the reference. Vibes-only theory is not a catalog entry.
decision.impact: Project market listing requirement: must have build or artifact set.

decision.title: Site reusability review needed â€” cut down on duplicate edits
decision.summary: The site needs a reusability pass: shared patterns reimplemented across pages, duplicate components, data shape changes requiring edits everywhere. Pipeline-generated data should drive generic components. Queued for future session.
decision.impact: Reduces maintenance burden as eval count grows to hundreds.

decision.title: GAD distribution is GitHub-first: executable releases + skills.sh, not npm publish
decision.summary: GAD should not rely on public npm publication as the primary distribution path. The official install story is: (1) download a GitHub Release executable for the gad operational CLI, and (2) install the published GAD skills from the GitHub repo via skills.sh / `npx skills add`. Users who already have Node may optionally install or run the CLI directly from the GitHub repo, but that is a secondary convenience path, not the canonical onboarding. Existing docs that assume `npx get-anything-done@latest` from the public npm registry are now stale.
decision.impact: Quick start, install docs, and release messaging must move from npm-first to GitHub-first. Release assets target standalone executables. Skill installation instructions point at skills.sh + GitHub repo installs.

decision.title: Executable release overhead is gated to shipped CLI surface changes only
decision.summary: Building and publishing new GAD executables should happen only when the shipped CLI surface changes. Triggering inputs include changes under bin/, lib/, hooks/, package.json, build/release scripts, or any file bundled into the released artifact. Site content, eval results, planning docs, starters, and other non-shipped repo changes must not force a new executable release. Windows remains the primary authoritative local release build; macOS and Linux can be built in GitHub Actions or locally when needed.
decision.impact: Release automation and operator workflow become path-aware. The project avoids paying binary rebuild cost on ordinary docs/site/eval commits while still enforcing executable rebuilds for real CLI changes.

decision.title: Runtime identity in eval telemetry is runtime-derived first, self-declared second
decision.summary: Eval telemetry must distinguish the coding agent runtime that produced a run: Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, and future supported agents. The preferred source is runtime-derived metadata from hooks, config, or environment. A secondary self-declared identity may be recorded by the agent at task/session start, but self-report alone is not sufficient for trustworthy evaluation data. TRACE.json and related logs should preserve runtime name, model when available, token/tool/skill/agent usage, and provenance for how the identity was determined. Claude Code and Codex are the first-class targets; the schema should remain extensible for additional runtimes.
decision.impact: TRACE.json, raw logs, and reporting need explicit runtime fields and provenance. Hook wiring and install flows must preserve enough metadata to attribute runs to a specific coding agent runtime so Claude vs Codex comparisons are trustworthy.

decision.title: Standalone gad executables use Node SEA with an injected support payload
decision.summary: The executable distribution for gad is implemented as a standalone binary built with Node SEA and postject. The binary embeds a packaged support payload containing the CLI bundle, installer entrypoint, hooks, commands, templates, workflows, references, agents, and skills. On first run, the executable extracts that payload into a versioned runtime directory under the user's profile and dispatches the extracted CLI or installer from there. End users do not need Node installed to run the packaged gad executable.
